data.title: Oxford start-up backed by CIA plots British Starlink rival
data.description:
An Oxford start-up backed by the CIA is preparing to build hundreds of satellites a year as it seeks to rival Elon Musk’s Starlink.

OpenCosmos, a British satellite-maker backed by investors including the CIA’s venture capital fund, announced plans on Monday for a new orbital network called ConnectedCosmos – which it said would counter Europe’s “over-reliance on transcontinental mega-constellations”.

Rafel Jorda Siquier, the founder and chief executive, said the start-up would soon have capacity at factories including its main facility in Harwell, near Oxford, to make nearly 200 satellites per year.

“We have the ability to deliver up to 150, soon close to 200, satellites per year,” he said. “In Harwell in particular, we now have capacity for manufacturing on average a satellite every three days.”

Mr Siquier said the start-up could provide a sovereign alternative for European governments and telecoms companies, which until now have been forced to turn to Mr Musk’s Starlink for critical communications.

Starlink, which is made up of thousands of small satellites orbiting the world, has been widely used to support the conflict in Ukraine by providing connectivity to front line forces and the government.

However, officials in Europe have become increasingly wary of relying on Starlink, a division of SpaceX, because of the mercurial nature of Mr Musk.

Mr Siquier said: “It’s very important that we don’t depend on systems where decisions are being taken by a single individual on the other side of the Atlantic.

“We want to be there as a very reliable solution. Of course for the UK and of course for across Europe – and also to countries that want to partner and collaborate without having to depend on the US or China.”

Europe has been eyeing building its own sovereign satellite network, called Iris 2, but it is expected to cost billions of euros and not be ready until 2030.

In January, OpenCosmos secured key spectrum rights for low-orbit satellites from regulators in Liechtenstein that could allow it to compete with Mr Musk’s SpaceX. The licence gives it the right to launch up to 288 satellites for its network by a deadline of 2028.

“It took many by surprise we had reserved this spectrum that is worth billions,” Mr Siquier said.

Mr Siquier announced OpenCosmos’s plans for its new satellite network at Mobile World Congress, a gathering of telecoms industry leaders in Barcelona.

Separately Vodafone struck a deal with Jeff Bezos’s satellite company that will help improve mobile coverage in remote, rural areas.

The telecoms giant will use Amazon Leo’s network of satellites in low Earth orbit to help connect calls, texts and data to mobile masts in areas where it is too difficult or expensive to install fibre cables, which usually underpin networks.

In addition to tackling mobile “not-spots” in rural areas, Vodafone will also use the technology to improve network resilience for emergency calls and critical online services if fibre cables are damaged or affected by flooding.

The partnership will be rolled out across Europe, including the UK, from this year. It will also be deployed in Africa.

OpenCosmos launched the first satellites that will form part of its new network in January in partnership with New Zealand launch business Rocket Lab.

Last week, SpaceIntelReport disclosed that OpenCosmos was seeking to raise up to $200m (£160m) to fund its expansion plans.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘Everyone is getting beaten with a stick’: Tradesmen turn their backs on Britain
data.description:
Vince Towns never planned to down his tools and leave Britain at the age of 51.

Having worked as a tradesman for 24 years, he hoped he would continue going for at least another decade so that he could pass on everything he knew to his two teenage sons.

But tax rises, soaring costs and suffocating bureaucracy have pushed him towards the exit long before his time.

“It’s just not worth me doing it,” he says. “Why should I bother? More and more is being taken away from me. I’ve lost so much heart in it now that I don’t want to put my body through it any more.”

Towns is making arrangements to move from Nottingham to Spain as soon as his wife obtains her Irish passport. When he gets there, he is considering continuing his work in construction.

And he thinks other tradesmen will soon follow him. “Everyone is getting beaten with a stick. People are earning just enough to live and you’ve seen how many businesses are going under – 2026 will be a big year for builders dropping out,” he says.

As Labour pursues its ambitious target of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, it has a real problem on its hands. To achieve its housebuilding dream, it needs an additional 300,000 construction workers in a range of trades from carpenters and bricklayers to plumbers, plasterers and roofers.

But not only are older workers throwing in the towel, younger workers are also seeking opportunities abroad, put off by punitive government policies, particularly on small businesses.

Since the start of Covid, Britain’s construction workforce has shrunk by 280,000 to just above two million, its lowest level in almost 25 years.

Meanwhile, other countries’ workforces have grown. In the same period, Australia added 183,000 tradesmen and builders, equivalent to a 15pc jump.

As the UK struggles to hold on to its workers, Chancellor Rachel Reeves’s decision to raise taxes for businesses threatens to accelerate the exodus and leave Britain trapped in a construction crisis for generations to come.

‘You get a better life back in Romania’

The foundations of Britain’s current construction industry crisis were laid around more than a decade ago as the world was still recovering from the global financial crisis.

Housebuilding starts had collapsed from around 180,000 before the crash to less than 80,000 by 2009, wiping out companies and triggering hundreds of thousands of job losses. As activity began to pick up again, however, construction firms increasingly turned to skilled migrant workers rather than invest in training up apprentices.

Professor Iain Begg, an expert on Eastern European economics at LSE, says the trend was fuelled by Poland’s entry into the EU in 2004, followed by Romania and Bulgaria in 2007, which opened the door to an influx of cheap labour.

“Employers could suddenly say, ‘I can hire a Pole rather than train a Brit’,” he says.

By 2016, non-UK-born workers made up 14.2pc of the entire sector, having comprised just 7pc in 2007. In London, this figure was as high as 40pc, according to the Office for National Statistics.

However, following the Brexit vote in 2016 and the shock of the pandemic, the inflow of migrants that had defined the previous decade went into reverse. The number of Poles in the UK fell from a peak of just over a million in 2017 to 698,000 four years later.

For many Eastern Europeans working in Britain, the twin effects of Brexit and Covid became an “inflection point”, says Prof Begg. “It motivated quite a lot of Poles in the UK to say, ‘okay, I’ve been here, done that, now is the time for me to move on and do something new’.”

Because the construction industry heavily relied on skilled migrants, it was disproportionately hit by the exodus and exposed a chronic lack of training for UK workers.

Yet even as the dust has settled on Brexit and the pandemic, foreign-born workers are continuing to up sticks as taxes and rising costs take the shine off higher salaries.

Florin Napa, a 27-year-old carpenter who moved to the UK from Romania in 2018, says he is planning on moving back after seeing a growing proportion of his income go to the taxman.

“Before it was fine because I could save money,” says Napa, who shared an apartment with several other foreign workers during his first few years in the UK. “When I arrived I wanted to buy a nice car and do something with my life.

“But I feel like now [as a labourer] if you don’t push yourself, work weekends and longer hours, longer days, it’s very hard to save money. Because the more you earn the more you pay [in tax].”

At the same time, wages and living standards in eastern European and Balkan countries have improved since they joined the EU. In 1990, for example, the GDP per capita in the UK was three times more than Romania; today the gap has closed to just 1.2.

This has convinced many like Napa that they will be better off back home. “You can now earn good money back in my country doing what I’m doing,” he says. “Carpenters can earn €4,000 a month, they have their own house, they don’t pay a mortgage. You’ve got a better life.”

‘Politicians don’t seem to understand how our industry operates’

Much of the blame falls on governments that have for years squeezed small builders and businesses. But recent Labour policies affecting the cost of hiring staff have made conditions particularly tough.

Since coming into office, Reeves has raised both employer National Insurance and the minimum wage. This includes a jump in apprentice wages from £6.40 in 2024 to £8 from April this year.

At the same time rising material costs and shrinking pipelines of work as projects get held up in planning purgatory are squeezing profits to breaking point.

Rico Wojtulewicz, of the National Federation of Builders, says as a result it has become increasingly difficult for small construction businesses to recruit and train young workers.

“The Government sets the environment for business to succeed or fail,” he says. “If the industry is continually seen as a cash cow, and the Government thinks it can keep piling on costs or bureaucracy or tax, then those people are going to struggle to run their businesses.”

While big housing developers can typically ride out such pressures, small businesses, which provide jobs for 80pc of UK apprentices, are buckling under the strain.

There are fewer jobs for tradesmen: in the construction industry vacancies have fallen fallen 32pc in the last three months to 28,000, the largest percentage decline across all sectors.

When those entering a trade cannot see a secure career path for themselves, Wojtulewicz says it is inevitable they will either move abroad or leave the profession altogether. “Politicians don’t seem to understand how our industry operates,” he says. “You can train a million people to do the jobs but if there aren’t a million career opportunities, then you’re wasting people’s time.”

‘You’re going to see a lot of the tradesmen of a certain age disappear’

Faced with diminishing career prospects, many young British labourers have moved to Australia, which has sought to tackle its own skills shortages by offering a A$10,000 visa grant scheme for UK tradesmen.

Between 2022 and 2024, the number of Australian visas issued to British carpenters and joiners more than doubled from 66 to 135, while the number of civil engineering professionals jumped from 72 to 179.

Charles Esterling-Cooper, who runs a relocation agency in Perth, says generous salaries, nice weather and a perception around better opportunities are helping to attract British labourers. “Skilled tradespeople often find jobs quickly, sometimes even before they get here,” he says.

“A lot of the tradespeople I talk to are moving with young families – and they’re coming for the long term.” He adds that only one of his clients has moved back to the UK in the last few years.

At the opposite end of the career ladder, the Making Tax Digital scheme is convincing some older workers to put down their tools early.

Under the new rules, self-employed workers with a gross income of £50,000 will be required to file quarterly tax reports using HMRC-compatible software. Currently they only need to submit one self-assessment form every tax year.

More than a third of the construction workforce is over the age of 50, meaning the sector is already facing a retirement cliff edge. Towns, who is preparing for his move to Spain, says it will pile on administrative costs and push others like him to quit the industry.

“To them, it’s a major hindrance. So you’re going to see a lot of the tradesmen of a certain age disappear and move into different things.”

And for experienced builders who do stay, Towns says there is little incentive to grow their businesses because of “nonsensical” tax thresholds, such as when VAT is triggered at a revenue of £90,000.

“There’s no point in going over that,” he says. “If you’re inefficient then the costs are going to outstrip any profit that you’re going to make. They’ve taken away the incentive to work.”

‘Construction underpins so much success in the country’

The consequences of Britain’s depleted construction industry workforce extend far beyond falling short of bold housebuilding pledges.

Skilled tradesmen are crucial for everything from kitchen extensions to vast public infrastructure projects. Without them, it creates an insurmountable hurdle that can have a damaging knock-on effect for other parts of the economy.

“Construction underpins so much success in the country, particularly growth,” says Wojtulewicz. “It’s not just 1.5 million homes, it’s all the stuff alongside it: the roads, the rail, the commercial, the retail, the amenities – all this stuff actually requires skilled labour.”

If the demand for such work remains, yet the workforce diminishes, it can also present an opportunity for less competent labourers to pick up jobs, warns Jeremy Gray, of trade association the Federation of Master Builders.

“Because you don’t have to have any minimum competence to be a builder, there are a lot of rogues out stealing business,” he says. “We’re getting more reports about a lot of remediation.”

The FMB estimates that over the last five years alone, homeowners have lost more than £14bn to unreliable builders.

Like Wojtulewicz, Gray also believes the country is being held back not just by a lack of new apprentices coming through but a distinct lack of opportunities for those that do.

The long-awaited Planning and Infrastructure Act, which came into law in December, may offer some reprieve for the construction industry.

For builders like Towns, however, it is far too late. After a quarter of a century of hard graft, he’s packing up his toolbox for the last time – with a lingering feeling of regret about how his career has ended.

“If I’d have known how things were going to change, I wouldn’t have done all that work and put my body through the stress,” he says.

“Your natural instinct is to work hard, have a family, educate them, save a bit for them and help them along. That’s the natural progression and it feels like it’s going backwards.”

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “This Government inherited a dire shortage of construction workers, and we are determined to deliver more opportunities for young people as we work to boost construction skills.

“We have committed £625m to recruit an additional 60,000 construction workers and £1.5bn to get hundreds of thousands of young people earning or learning, including in construction.

“We’re leaving no stone unturned to build 1.5 million homes and we’re already seeing green shoots of recovery with an 18pc increase in housing starts last year.”

data.title: Staff don’t want to leave Dubai, says Savills
data.description:
Savills has said its 800 staff working in Gulf states do not want to leave despite the mounting conflict.

Simon Shaw, chief executive of the British property company, said that there were no plans in place “at the moment” to evacuate staff if the conflict persists.

“Nor is anybody wanting to be evacuated, if I’m being completely honest,” he said.

Banks and finance companies including PwC, Deloitte, Citigroup and Standard Chartered have reportedly urged staff to evacuate offices in Dubai. HSBC is said to have closed branches in Qatar temporarily.

The corporate exodus was prompted by a threat from Iran that it would target economic centres and banks in the region linked to the US and Israel, after one of its major banks was hit in Tehran.

Mr Shaw said that none of its staff was proactively looking to leave offices or buildings that Savills manages on behalf of clients in the Gulf.

Its staff are based at offices in Abu Dhabi, Bahrain, Dubai, Egypt, Oman, Saudi Arabia and Sharjah, as well as Pakistan. Its Middle East operations accounted for around 5pc of the overall group’s pre-tax profit last year.

“What we’ve done is taken the advice of both the Foreign Office and every local government in the regions in which we operate, and following that,” said Mr Shaw.

“The Middle East management team is 100pc focused on making sure that people are safe.”

‘Business as usual’

Mr Shaw added that many of those employees were property management staff working at the sites of buildings belonging to clients. He said the sentiment on the ground was “positive” and that Savills would continue to invest in the region.

“It is business as usual, but of course everybody is being careful,” he said.

He emphasised that it was difficult to assess what the impact of the conflict in the Middle East would be on the industry as the situation is changing “hour by hour”, but that property deals are still happening.

“The London market and certain European markets have always been very favoured markets for Middle Eastern capital, and I don’t think anything will change at all in that,” added Mr Shaw.

It comes as Savills prepares to spend £685m on buying Eastdil Secured, a renowned property-focused investment bank, in a move that will significantly boost its presence in the US market.

“This deal will supercharge our business for a generation,” said Mr Shaw.

Revenue at Savills rose by more than 6pc to nearly £2.6bn in the year ending December 2025, while pre-tax profit jumped by 14pc to £101m, withstanding what Mr Shaw called “the well-rehearsed challenges of tariffs and fiscal uncertainty”.

Shares in Savills fell by as much as 8.7pc in Thursday morning trading.

data.title: Britain’s housing crisis is Labour’s choice – it’s in thrall to Nimbys
data.description:
Everyone knows London needs more homes. The city has become unaffordable for almost everyone because it has essentially stopped building.

Just 4,550 homes a year are expected to be completed in 2027 and 2028, according to Molior data. That is about 5pc of the 90,000 homes London needs annually, according to government targets. Some boroughs are projected to complete zero homes over the next two years. London is building less than any other global city.

And the reasons are not mysterious. They are political and bureaucratic, rules stacked on top of rules until nothing can get through the planning system. At the national level, the Building Safety Regulator has made approval for buildings higher than 18 metres slow, uncertain and in practice close to impossible. In a city where height is often the only way to build at scale, that is catastrophic.

Sadiq Khan’s London Plan imposes a thicket of requirements that do not exist elsewhere in the country, such as rules on “dual aspect” homes, which are intended to improve cooling and ventilation even while London bans air conditioning for the vast majority of residential developments. The result is fewer, more expensive homes. That’s before London councils pile their own rules on top.

Islington is a prime example of this. It is meant to deliver about 2,400 of London’s annual target of 90,000 new homes. In practice, it is projected to build about 100 each year in 2027 and 2028, according to Molior.

All councils face organised opposition from local “Nimby” campaigns. But Islington council, confronted with relatively modest resistance, chose to retreat rather than lead.

The council proposed building 18 new homes on a slab of concrete it owned in Morton Road Park. To counteract the loss of open space, it proposed expanding the green bit and extending the park. The result would have been a small reduction in “open space” on paper, but an increase in actual green space on the ground.

The proposed development itself would have been four to five storeys, in keeping with the surrounding buildings. It sat around the corner from Essex Road station and about a 15-minute walk from the Tube. Even by Islington’s standards, Canonbury is expensive. If there is anywhere in London where a small infill scheme makes sense, it is here. It is hard to imagine a lower-impact development in a dense inner London neighbourhood.

But it never even reached the planning application stage. Instead, a campaign took off: signs appeared declaring “Save Morton Road Park”, as if the entire park were under threat. The concrete slab earmarked for housing was described as a “football pitch”.

On a recent visit, the playground and grass were full of children. The hardstanding was empty. While a football could technically be kicked around on it, calling it a football pitch is generous.

Campaign materials did not mention the additional green space that would have been created. They did not mention that the bit being built on was, in essence, a car park-like concrete pad.

There were other objections, including claims that removing the hardstanding would increase knife crime. Some nearby flats complained they might lose their view of the New Year’s Eve fireworks on the Thames, which is a few miles away.

But many were more straightforward. People opposed the development because it was happening near them.

In the end, the council caved, citing a petition signed by less than 3pc of residents.

According to the council’s own website, there are now zero housing developments under way in Canonbury. And unlike private schemes, there is no appeal route here. This was the council proposing homes on its own land. It could have built these 18 homes under the current planning system, but it chose not to.

These 18 homes would not have solved the housing crisis. But when even 18 modest homes on a patch of concrete are too much, it becomes much easier to understand why the crisis continues.

The rules and regulations on building in London are undeniably onerous. But the deeper truth is this: even where councils can build, many Labour councils in the heart of London are unwilling to contribute to the Government’s housing targets if it causes them any difficulty whatsoever.

Even if you don’t live in and have no plans to move to Islington, this affects you. Whether or not London builds, people will still want to live in places that can access London’s high-paying jobs. London’s housing crisis gets exported to the suburbs and rural areas.

If we don’t build in London, we will have to concrete over the countryside. Wouldn’t it be better to concrete over concrete?

Michael Hill is a policy researcher at Britain Remade, a campaign to grow Britain’s economy by building the power, homes and transport that we need

data.title: Vistry boss exits as pivot to social housing sparks £500m crash
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest housebuilders has seen around £500m wiped off its value in the wake of the surprise exit of its boss.

Shares in FTSE 250 housebuilder Vistry Group plunged by as much as 24pc in early trading as it announced Greg Fitzgerald’s departure after his radical pivot to affordable housing failed to bear fruit.

The company warned that its profit growth in 2026 is unlikely to meet expectations.

Mr Fitzgerald blamed the disappointing numbers on uncertainty and speculation around last November’s Budget for the decline.

Vistry staked its bets on a so-called partnerships model, which focuses on building homes on behalf of housing associations, local authorities and private rented sector landlords rather than selling them on the open market like many of its rivals.

However, the builder’s partner-funded forward order book dropped by 10pc to £3.7bn in the year ending December 2025, with Vistry citing a slowdown in contracts.

Partner funded-homes, which accounted for nearly three quarters of total completions, dropped by 8pc during the year, as housing associations paused funding and some private rental housing partners refinanced.

Revenue at the housebuilder fell by 4pc to £4.1bn, while total completions dropped by 9pc to 15,658 homes.

Known for his charisma, Mr Fitzgerald took the helm at the company after Bovis Homes took over Galliford Try’s housebuilding business, which he ran, in a £1.1bn deal. Bovis was later rebranded as Vistry. 

After more than 45 years in the property industry, Mr Fitzgerald said it was “the right time” to leave.

His strategy to pivot to building most of its homes for partners in 2023 was well-regarded by many in the industry.

However, it later emerged that build costs at Vistry’s nine projects had been understated by £165m, leading to a string of profit warnings. More than £1bn was wiped off the value of Vistry in 2024.

Anthony Codling, analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said that Mr Fitzgerald’s strategy to shift Vistry into a partnership model was a “bold and logical move given the dire shortage of social and affordable homes”.

“However, such a change is a paradigm shift, and the execution of the strategy has, so far, not lived up to the group’s bold aspirations,” he said.

“The need for social and affordable housing remains acute, and we may look back with hindsight and conclude that Mr Fitzgerald’s move ... was a little early.”

The builder warned that its profit margins will be lower since it will need to increase its use of incentives to tempt more buyers and boost sales. These include helping buyers with deposits and stamp duty contributions.

However, Vistry said it expects to see sales growth and improved cash flow in the second half of the year.

Mr Fitzgerald is stepping down as executive chairman of the housebuilder in May while retaining the chief executive role for up to a year, or until his successor is found.

Elsewhere in the sector, Barratt Redrow said David Thomas, its chief executive, would retire after 11 years at the business. He will be replaced by Dean Banks, currently group chief executive of infrastructure provider Ventia.

data.title: Seven ways to help your children buy their first home
data.description:
Getting on the property ladder has become increasingly difficult over the years. House price growth has long outstripped wages, while rising rents have inhibited tenants’ ability to save, and stricter lending criteria have compounded the problem.

Almost a third of first-time buyers now rely on financial support to buy a home, according to figures from Barclays. And, for most, this means making an often sizeable withdrawal from the Bank of Mum and Dad.

But it can be tricky for parents to work out the best way to approach this and how much they can realistically afford to give.

Here, Telegraph Money explains the main ways to support first-time buyers, including the pros and cons, so you can decide what’s right for your family.

1. Pay into a Lifetime Isa

If your child is aged 18 or over and buying a home is a future aspiration in a few years, encouraging them to open a Lifetime Isa (Lisa) now and offering to contribute to it is a good step.

Anyone aged 18 to 39 can open these accounts and save up to £4,000 a year to put towards their first home or retirement. The big incentive is that the Government adds a 25pc bonus, capped at £1,000 a year.

Richard Dana, of mortgage and savings platform Tembo, said: “If a parent were to support their child from the age of 18 to 28 with the full annual contribution to a Lisa, that would bolster their child’s deposit by £10,000 in bonuses alone. 

“When including compounding interest gains, this accumulates further into a very meaningful sum.”

This could be a good option for those who don’t have a large lump sum sitting waiting for their child, but who can afford to contribute to their child’s savings each tax year.

Lisa funds can be held as cash or invested in stocks and shares, and all interest growth or investment gains are tax-free.

It’s worth noting the Government’s plan to replace the Lifetime Isa in 2028 with an alternative savings product geared towards first-time buyers. It’s understood that anyone who already has a Lisa will be able to continue saving with it after this time.

2. Give a lump sum as a gift

If your child dreams of buying a home soon and you have the means, giving a lump-sum gift is a straightforward way to boost their deposit. A larger deposit can make it easier to qualify for a mortgage and secure more competitive interest rates.

As it’s a gift, the money doesn’t need to be repaid. But this makes it crucial not to risk your own financial security or retirement plans by giving away more than you can afford.

Giving gifts can also have inheritance tax implications.

Tom Evans, of estate agency Purplebricks, explained: “This type of gift is known as a Potentially Exempt Transfer (PET), where if you die within seven years of granting the loan, some or all of it might fall back into your estate for inheritance tax.”

You may be able to reduce the tax risk by using available gift allowances, and it’s essential to keep accurate records of when the money was given.

Mortgage lenders will usually require written confirmation that the funds were a gift and that there is no need to repay them.

3. Provide an interest-free loan

Instead of gifting the money outright, you could provide an interest-free loan. This gives your child similar benefits to a cash gift, but the money must be repaid, limiting the financial impact on you.

From an inheritance tax perspective, this option means the loan remains part of the parents’ estate.

However, Mr Evans warned: “If you opt for the loan route, you need to be able to prove to the HMRC that it was always intended to be repaid. Otherwise, you risk it being reverted to being a gift, which would trigger PET rules.”

The best way to navigate this is to draw up a formal written agreement that outlines the repayment terms and confirms that the loan is interest-free. Both parties must sign it.

Mortgage lenders may be willing to accept this type of arrangement, provided the loan doesn’t need to be repaid immediately to avoid affordability issues.

Mr Evans said: “In practice, this often means your child will not be saddled with monthly repayments, plus the repayment can be deferred until the property is sold or remortgaged.”

4. Remortgage to release funds

Not everyone is fortunate enough to have readily available funds to give to their children. Remortgaging your own property to release cash can be an attractive choice if you have built up significant equity in your home, particularly if mortgage rates are low.

However, taking on additional borrowing later in life can be risky. Your monthly repayments could increase, putting extra strain on your finances, and lenders may restrict the amount you can borrow as you approach retirement.

Alexandra Loydon, of St James’s Place, said: “If you decide to go down this route, you need to be completely comfortable that you have the means by which to repay the mortgage and are happy to absorb the set-up costs.

“You then need to decide whether you’re lending or gifting the funds, or a combination of the two.”

5. Opt for equity release

Those over 55 could consider equity release instead. This enables you to unlock tax-free cash from your property’s value without moving – but it must be used with caution.

The most popular type of equity release is a lifetime mortgage. It’s designed to last your lifetime or until you move into permanent care.

Lifetime mortgages usually come with flexible payment options. This means you can choose to pay interest each month or not pay any interest and have it “rolled up” and added to the loan. When the property is sold, the loan is repaid in full.

The advantage of equity release is that you can help financially support your child while continuing to live in your home. But there are downsides to consider.

Mr Evans said: “Compounding interest can chip away at the value of an estate, making this an option best suited to families who want to provide immediate support over safeguarding future inheritance.”

Because interest builds over time, equity release can significantly reduce the value of what you leave behind. Releasing equity can also affect your entitlement to means-tested benefits. This makes it essential to seek financial advice first.

6. Downsize your home to free up cash

Selling a larger home and moving to a smaller property can seem like a neat way to free up the funds you require without taking on additional borrowing.

Helen Morrissey, of investment platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “You may feel like the family home is now too big for you and need something a bit smaller. You may believe you can release enough money to get that smaller home while releasing enough funds to give your loved one money towards a deposit.”

However, it’s important to do your sums. Moving can be expensive, especially when you factor in stamp duty, estate agent fees and solicitor fees.

Ms Morrissey added: “You also need to make sure that you can release enough money to meet your own housing needs, as well as those of your family members.”

7. Sign on to the mortgage

If it’s not possible to stump up a large cash gift or loan, release equity or move home, lenders such as NatWest and Skipton offer mortgage products aimed at parents wanting to help their children buy a property.

Often known as Joint Borrower, Sole Proprietor (JBSP) mortgages, they allow up to four people to be named on the mortgage.

Phil Spencer, TV property expert and founder of property advice website Move iQ, said: “The principle is that the parents and their adult children borrow the money together, thus allowing everyone’s income to be taken into account.

“The child will own the property – that’s the ‘sole proprietor’ bit – but because they’re borrowing alongside their parents, the lender will lend significantly more than they would if only the child’s income were included.”

Unlike a traditional joint mortgage, where all parties’ names appear on the deeds, JBSP mortgages keep ownership separate from support. However, all borrowers on the mortgage are liable if repayments are not made, so this type of mortgage still carries risk.

Mr Dana added: “Lenders will also assess the finances of everyone involved, meaning existing debts, mortgage commitments or approaching retirement can limit eligibility.”

If you are considering a JSBP mortgage, it’s best to consider it as a temporary arrangement. This means that once your child can afford the mortgage on their own, the parent should be removed from it.

data.title: ‘Rogue builders ran off with £200k and left me homeless’
data.description:
Have you lost money to rogue builders? Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Steve Higgs never envisioned that six years after buying his forever home in Surrey, he would find himself living in a plasterboard shack and £200,000 out of pocket.

But when the building company he hired to redevelop his £625,000 property claimed it had gone bust and abruptly ceased all communication with him, he was left with nothing but foundations and a few half-finished walls despite handing over £170,000 for the work.

Mr Higgs, 39, had set out to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom property in Horley, after he was advised that it had serious asbestos issues.

Since early 2022, he has been forced to live in a makeshift cabin on the grounds of his three-acre estate as his property became uninhabitable.

He last had contact with the building company responsible in January 2025 when they told him they were experiencing “financial troubles” after his home had been demolished.

Mr Higgs, who was paralysed from the chest down in a motorcycle accident when he was 26, said the ordeal had left him emotionally drained, homeless and facing a big hole in his finances.

He said: “It has played havoc on my mental health. They’ve already wasted four years of my life and £170,000. I can’t sit on my hands in this little box any more. I’ve got no windows. It’s just plasterboard walls. I’m literally just living in a cell of my own making.”

It comes as Britain faces a plague of rogue builders that is costing homeowners and the economy billions of pounds a year.

In the past five years alone, homeowners lost an estimated £14.3bn to cowboy builders, while a fear of being ripped off has cost the economy a further £10bn in lost jobs, according to the Federation of Master Builders (FMB) trade body.

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, said the cost of living crisis had created the “perfect environment” for sham building companies to proliferate.

He said: “There are no barriers to entry so it’s easy to come in and scam people. The rogue builder will come along, undercut a decent builder and then, because people are short of cash, they will go for the cheap option. And it’s only getting worse.”

Mr Higgs paid the building company £113,000 to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom house in Horley, Surrey, after he was advised that issues with asbestos and rotten timber frames made it more cost-effective than his original plan to renovate and extend.

He paid an additional £55,000 for roof truss installation and spent £30,000 on buying materials directly from a roofing supplier.

He said the building company had a strong online presence, including a professional website, Facebook reviews and a range of photos displaying previous work, as well as a branded fleet of vans. Up to four labourers a day would appear on site.

However, when Mr Higgs raised concerns about the quality of bricklaying and incorrect proportions for the new foundations, he was told, in correspondence seen by The Telegraph, that the company had “run into financial troubles”.

Despite numerous calls and emails the company stopped responding to queries about when the remedial work would be carried out.

After a few months, he hired a lawyer to draft a letter before action for £7,000, only to be advised that it could cost up to £120,000 pursuing the claim in a civil court.

He was told that even if he won there was no guarantee he would be able to recoup what he paid out as the company was claiming financial distress.

‘Hopeless’ legal process

Currently all builders carrying out work on private homes are regulated by the Consumer Rights Act 2015. It sets out the standards that consumers can expect when a trader supplies building works, and statutory remedies if those rights are breached.

However, if the company is declared insolvent, options for reclaiming money can be extremely limited and outcomes are typically poor.

Mark Garnier, shadow economic secretary, who has campaigned for more stringent controls on building firms, has previously called the legal process for bringing rogue traders to justice “hopeless”.

If the work was paid for using a credit card, homeowners can claim back up to £30,000 under Section 75. But many rogue traders encourage cash-in-hand payments at a discount to avoid creating a paper trail.

Paula Higgins, at the Homeowners’ Alliance trade body, said “phoenix companies” – whereby the owner of an insolvent business simply rebrands under a different name – was an ongoing problem in the construction sector.

She said: “If you investigate certain directors, they will be setting companies up and closing them down all the time – [the regulation] around it is very weak.”

Despite calls from industry leaders for tighter regulation such as a mandatory national licensing scheme ensuring minimum competency for all builders, the Government has dragged its heels on introducing any new legislation.

Mr Gray said the Government didn’t appear to have any plans to implement controls in the construction sector despite “zero protections” for consumers against rogue builders.

He said: “Anyone can call themselves a builder because they’re not regulated. In certain circumstances you can go through the court, but ultimately if the company folds there’s nothing you can do. You can’t claim anything back. You just have to go to the drawing board.”

To avoid falling prey to rogue builders, Ms Higgins said the most important steps were to get a contract in place, avoid paying for projects upfront and in cash, and to carry out extensive research into the company beyond its own website and social media channels.

She also warned against any company that is available at short notice. “The best builders are the ones you have to wait for,” she said, adding that everyone should look on Companies House to see for how long a business has been operating.

For word of mouth recommendations, ensure their previous work is similar to what you are asking for and check previous projects yourself, she added.

Mr Higgs said he had been quoted £23,000 for remedial work, including widening the existing foundation to the correct size. The rest of the project is expected to cost another £90,000, plus VAT.

“It’s pretty much looking at the same figures again,” he said.

Mr Higgs receives an income from a personal injury trust, but said the financial impact would be long-lasting. “It’s definitely thrown a spanner in the works,” he said. “It’s turned what was meant to be a five-year build plan into a 10-year plan.”

A spokesman for the Department for Business and Trade said: “Rogue builders are a scourge for hard-working families. Anyone who is affected can contact their local authority who have new powers to hand out tougher fines against businesses who rip off consumers.”

data.title: Reeves accused of putting vital South West rail route at risk
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has been accused of putting South West England’s main rail route at risk by limiting maintenance spending while promising billions for Northern projects.

Fears are mounting that a sea cliff could collapse on to the line in Devon, cutting off Plymouth and the whole of Cornwall, after recent storms saw a sinkhole near the railway in Dawlish.

The cliff in question had been earmarked for £400m of earthworks after a 2014 collapse closed the line for eight weeks, costing the local economy £1.2bn.

However, the plan was abandoned after Labour sought to cut costs across the UK’s rail network. Instead, ministers suggested the cliff would be monitored for signs of movement. 

Storm Ingrid, which battered Devon two weeks ago, also saw waves destroy sections of a sea wall protecting the tracks at Dawlish, closing the line for two days.

Rebecca Smith, Tory MP for South West Devon, said the damage had stoked concerns that spending cuts had left the lifeline route exposed to potential disaster.

She said: “With all the tightening of the budget by the Chancellor, she has taken away contingency funding. So if there were something that needed an emergency reaction, the implication is that the money isn’t actually there.

“If you get the right kind of freakish storm, who knows what could happen. The question for me is, should this not be something that is in capital funding on a year-on-year basis?”

After Ms Smith asked in the Commons whether funding could be guaranteed for unplanned resilience work, James Murray, the chief secretary to the Treasury, said that budgets allow ministers to respond to emergencies.

He said: “All individual departments must recognise that unexpected pressures will come along; they need to prepare for them and make sure they have robust plans to respond.”

Ms Smith, a member of the transport select committee, said the Government’s reluctance to fund work on the cliff stood in contrast to its backing for the £45bn Northern Powerhouse Rail project, for which Ms Reeves last month announced £1bn of preparatory funding.

She said: “Why do they want to take a gamble when they are investing billions in other parts of the country? Why do we need a marginally faster rail system in the North when in the Southwest, if this went again, we’d be cut off for, I don’t know how long?”

Ms Smith also raised doubts about the level of monitoring that Network Rail is carrying out on the cliff, which is protected by the railway from the sea but prone to becoming sodden with surface water.

She said: “Are they monitoring it or just draining things? We need answers if they are not prepared to put the investment in to fix it.”

Martin Wrigley, Liberal Democrat MP for Newton Abbot, which includes Dawlish, said that had Storm Ingrid hit when seas were higher, the damage could have been much more severe.

He said the Department for Transport (DfT) should grant Network Rail money to undertake a study into a long-term solution for the cliff.

He said: “Monitoring is no good because the last collapse was a single, catastrophic failure, almost a tectonic shift of part of the cliff which came down on to the railway.”

Mr Wrigley said the damage to the Dawlish sea wall was also concerning, having affected areas on either side of a new section installed after the 2014 collapse.

The DfT said work carried out after the 2014 events held up well in the recent storms, with damage limited to a low wall between the coastal path and the railway. 

It said funding had been provided for a drainage trial and a range of advanced monitoring equipment, including tilt sensors that would provide a quick response if triggered.

Work will continue with Network Rail to assess the investment needed to ensure the line’s resilience and to develop any longer-term solutions.

data.title: Telegraph takeover by Axel Springer to be resolved ‘without delay’
data.description:
The £575m takeover of The Telegraph by Axel Springer has moved into the regulatory process just days after it was agreed.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, said it was of “paramount importance to me that a resolution is reached without further delay” as “the ongoing uncertainty is detrimental to the stability of The Telegraph and particularly for its staff”.

She received a formal application from RedBird IMI on Friday for permission to transfer the security over The Telegraph, which it has held since December 2023, to Axel Springer.

The financial instrument is designed to be immediately converted into full ownership.

RedBird IMI, a venture mostly funded by the United Arab Emirates, was blocked from carrying out the move by new laws banning foreign state influence over newspapers.

Ms Nandy said she will “thoroughly assess” the effect that the German publisher’s proposed acquisition may have on public interest considerations. Under special laws that apply only to media takeovers, they include press plurality, free expression of opinion and accurate presentation of news.

She said the deal will also be looked at under the foreign state influence laws. Axel Springer said on Friday that it believes the transaction is “fully compliant” with the regime.

The process may require preliminary review by the Competition and Markets Authority and Ofcom.

In contrast to the Daily Mail publisher DMGT, which was in the lead to buy The Telegraph until Friday, Axel Springer’s plans are not expected to raise plurality concerns demanding investigation. Its decades as a leading news publisher committed to editorial independence may also speed up the review.

The company, which owns German titles Bild and Die Welt as well as Politico and Business Insider, has agreed an all-cash deal for The Telegraph. It marks by far its biggest move into the UK.

For Mathias Döpfner, Axel Springer’s chief executive and controlling shareholder, the deal achieves an ambition held since at least 2004, when he was outbid in an auction of The Telegraph.

He outbid DMGT, which had agreed to buy The Telegraph in November. The apparent pace of developments since Axel Springer’s bid last week is much greater. Lord Rothermere, DMGT’s owner, had been expected to face several months of regulatory scrutiny.

The DMGT transaction faced regulatory scrutiny on competition and media plurality grounds. A lack of payment led to DMGT’s period of exclusivity expiring, allowing Axel Springer to swoop in with its £575m bid.

A swift conclusion would come as relief to staff. The Telegraph has been in limbo since June 2023, when the Barclay family lost control to Lloyds Banking Group over £1.2bn in overdue debts.

Mr Döpfner said last week: “We want to grow The Telegraph, while preserving its distinctive character and legacy, to help it become the most-read and intellectually inspiring centre-Right media outlet in the English-speaking world.

“The Telegraph stands for freedom, personal responsibility, democratic values and a belief in open societies and market economies.”

data.title: Killer motorists should be treated like the murderers they are
data.description:
April 25 2007 is etched forever in my mind. It was a beautiful day, and summer was just around the corner. My twin sister had given birth to a baby boy, and my 65-year-old mother, Elizabeth, had come to stay with me in Mortlake, south London. We planned to go to the Chelsea and Westminster Hospital to visit the new baby.

My husband walked my two eldest children, Barnaby and Sapphire – who were six and four at the time – to school and then hopped on a train to work. My mother, my two-year-old daughter, Pollyanna, and I set off to catch the bus to the hospital.

My mother, who lived in rural Shropshire, loved London buses and much preferred them to the train.

We walked, with Pollyanna in her pram, the short distance from our house to the local bus depot. As we approached, Pollyanna climbed out of her buggy and held my mother’s hand.

This was when my life changed irreversibly. I turned around, and all I could see was a monstrous, red Metroline bus approaching at a ferocious speed. Instead of turning into the depot, it drove on to the pavement, aiming straight for us. It happened so fast – almost like a bomb going off.

My legs were trapped under the bus, and I was like the jam in the middle of a horrid sandwich – bus, me, wall. Blood was everywhere. I slipped in and out of consciousness. Out of one eye, I saw my darling, precious, beautiful mother slipping away. She did not survive.

Out of my other eye, I saw my little daughter about 20 ft away. She had been flung through the air, and her right leg was hanging off. I was trapped and could not help her. I shouted for my mother so loudly. I wanted her to answer me, but she did not. She could not.

The crash quickly flooded the news as my husband – The Telegraph’s Christopher Hope – received a call at work to tell him what had happened.

As he made the terrible journey from his office in Victoria to Mortlake, he did not even know if we were alive or not. Four surgeons spent seven hours trying to save Pollyanna’s leg, but they could not, and she had it amputated.

I suffered severe degloving in my leg (where skin and soft tissue is torn from the muscle or bone underneath), among other injuries. However, knowing that I would never see my mother again was the most unbearable wound.

The bus driver who hit us was found guilty of one count of death by dangerous driving and two counts of grievous bodily harm. He was sentenced to four years in prison, which is simply not long enough. Often, only aggravating factors like being under the influence of drink or drugs result in a longer sentence, but it is rare that criminal drivers get more than 12 years behind bars.

On average, four people die every day on UK roads, and many dozens more suffer life-changing injuries. It is no wonder that victims of road crime – like me – feel utterly let down by the UK’s criminal justice system.

Metroline would not speak to me when I rang up their head office. I felt upset, hopeless and angry. So, in February 2014, I set out on a mission – for road crime to be treated as real crime, and for victims and their families to be treated with kindness and compassion in navigating the criminal justice system.

I met Boris Johnson, who was the mayor of London at the time. He said he was very sorry for what had happened to my family. He asked me whether Transport for London (TfL) had apologised, and I said no.

He told me that must change. A few weeks later, my husband and I met with TfL and Metroline. They said they were very sorry. I said that “sorry” must hold meaning, and things began to change from then on.

The Sarah Hope Line was launched in February 2016. It is an incident line that offers confidential support to people involved in, or affected by, life-changing incidents on the TfL network. We have provided to more than 500 families what TfL did not give to us: trauma counselling, assistance with funeral costs and arrangements, and short-term accommodation for family members. Next Monday will mark our 10-year anniversary.

While the line supports families of the bereaved, we still have not yet changed the attitude within government that road crashes are “just one of those things”. This Government should spend much more on policing our roads, so that criminal drivers can be caught. We need to issue immediate fines and driving bans so motorists are deterred from driving dangerously.

Road crime and the damage it causes cost the taxpayer billions of pounds every year – it strains the NHS, criminal justice system and our emergency services. Fatalities, alone, cost the NHS billions every year. That money should be spent on policing our roads and preventing such devastation.

Perpetrators think they can get away with causing death by dangerous driving because sentences do not reflect the severity of the crime. Change must start now. We must no longer be the forgotten victims.

The Sarah Hope Line is on 0343 222 5678, Monday to Friday, 8am-6pm. For more details, go to https://tfl.gov.uk/travel-information/safety/incident-support-service

data.title: Labour hits ferries with new net zero tax
data.description:
Northern Ireland businesses are set to be hammered by a green tax which bosses fear will make them uncompetitive.

A new tax on ferries to Northern Ireland, set to be introduced this summer, will swell freight rates – weighing on the economy and driving up prices.

The levy system, known as the emissions trading scheme (ETS), stands to increase shipping costs to the province by 6pc when it comes into force in July, according industry estimates.

The UK Chamber of Shipping said that will put more pressure on an economy where supply chain costs are already higher than the rest of the UK because of arrangements imposed under Brexit’s Windsor Framework.

It said the tax will also add to the red tape foisted on business following the agreement, which placed Northern Ireland in the EU’s single market for goods after Brexit.

The ETS scheme requires shipping firms to pay for their CO2 emissions as part of the Government’s push to reach net zero by 2050.

Unlike other ferry routes, Northern Ireland will not be exempt from the charges, even though 90pc of commercial and passenger traffic goes by sea.

The Chamber said ferry firms will most likely have to pass the additional charges on.

Stena Line, which operates ferries to Belfast from Liverpool, Heysham in Lancashire and Cairnryan in Scotland, estimates that the scheme will increase its costs by £9m annually. That would average £15 for every HGV, van and car.

The Chamber said an initial 50pc deduction to match the carbon charges on voyages between Britain and Republic of Ireland will provide little relief.

That is because exporters in Northern Ireland compete mostly with firms in Britain rather than the province’s southern neighbour, to which they will be at a structural disadvantage.

UK firms have already spent millions on some of the most sustainably designed ferries, including methanol and rotor sail propulsion, leaving no financially viable way forward, it claimed.

The Chamber said: “The sector supports the UK’s climate goals, but cannot deliver meaningful emissions reduction without the necessary fuels, infrastructure, and clear guidance in place.

“Premature implementation risks higher costs for passengers and freight, with limited environmental gain. However, the Government is pressing ahead, leaving industry in an untenable position.”

The trade group urged ministers to delay introduction of the scheme while further assessments are carried out and said the revenue raised should be ring-fenced for investment in clean fuels and quayside power connections.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said that it is for ship operators to decide how much of the expense of the ETS scheme to pass on, and that consumers should not see significant costs.

A spokesman said: “Pricing emissions is a cost-effective way to cut carbon emissions and encourage greener travel. We are working closely with industry on implementation.”

data.title: Taxpayers foot bill to save £15m rail yard sunk by Reeves raid
data.description:
Labour is to spend taxpayers’ money on taking over a £15m rail-freight project abandoned by Eurotunnel after Rachel Reeves tripled the company’s business rates bill.

Network Rail is poised to step in to save a planned upgrade of the Barking Eurohub freight terminal in east London, with the project to be bankrolled with government money.

Eurotunnel walked away from the project and its other UK investments in November, when the Treasury revealed that its rates bill would jump from £22m to £65m.

The upgrade of the Barking Eurohub is viewed as vital to kickstarting cargo flows through the Channel Tunnel after only a handful of freight trains used the link last year.

The Department for Transport (DfT) said Network Rail, which is to be merged with nationalised train companies to form Great British Railways, would take control of the yard and its property development arm, Platform4. 

DfT described it as a “government-backed deal” for the Barking site, which is currently owned by Legal & General. 

Network Rail said it was in talks with potential future operators for the terminal, which could bring in £15m of investment, while “getting on with delivering the works required to reinvigorate the site and make it fit for purpose.”

A spokesman for Network Rail said the track and station operator would work to “transform the site into a cargo hub”.

Labour ‘driving away investment’

However, critics criticised the deal on Wednesday. Jerome Mayhew, the shadow rail minister, claimed that Network Rail was being forced to step in after the Chancellor’s “astronomical” business rates increase drove out Eurotunnel.

He said: “When business leaders and rail operators warned that these punitive increases would have real-world consequences, this is exactly what they meant.

“Labour’s own tax decisions have driven away investment and forced taxpayers to underwrite infrastructure that should have been delivered commercially.”

The DfT argued that the plan for the Barking terminal would revive the cross-Channel rail freight market and boost trade links with Europe, helping to stimulate the economy and reducing road congestion and emissions.

It said the key to the redevelopment would be to allow full-size European container trains restricted to the High Speed 1 route to reach London.

That would enable food markets to receive fruit and vegetables direct from overseas growers and open up the possibility of Scotch whisky and British consumer items being exported by rail.

Network Rail said it envisaged three trains a day running into the terminal from the Continent, connecting with services to Scotland via central England and the North West and removing 140,000 HGVs from the roads each year.

Maggie Simpson, the director general of the Rail Freight Group, said the Government’s intervention was necessary to “keep the dream of Channel Tunnel cargo alive” and safeguard a site whose loss would have prevented container trains from ever using the link.

However, she said that factors including unfair cost advantages that favour the trucking industry would need to be addressed if significant volumes of freight were to use the tunnel.

Ms Simpson said ministers were also compelled to step in because of the Barking yard’s importance to containerised defence-related shipments at a time of heightening international tension.

She said: “If you look at Britain’s strategic resilience it is a vital national asset for whatever you want to transport to and from Europe, and that includes defence shipments”.

The Barking site, originally developed by Deutsche Bahn’s DB Cargo for automotive shipments, received the first direct freight train from China to the UK in 2017, when a rake of 34 containers arrived there after an 18-day journey.

data.title: Asda hands out bonuses despite missing targets
data.description:
Asda is handing out bonuses to thousands of staff despite missing targets during a turnaround drive.

The supermarket told 9,500 workers, who include head office staff and senior leaders in stores, that they would all receive a thank-you payment at the end of this month.

In a memo sent to staff, seen by The Telegraph, Asda chairman Allan Leighton said all salaried workers who had started before October 2025 would receive a bonus to reflect their “hard work throughout the year”.

It comes despite some teams missing targets for the bonuses.

Mr Leighton said, in certain parts of its business, “not all metrics were fully achieved”. However, he added that “there was still significant effort, commitment and progress made under challenging circumstances”.

Workers who met their objectives for the year will receive their entire bonuses, while those who did not will still get just over a quarter of what they would have received. The amount each worker receives will depend on their grade.

Mr Leighton said: “While we didn’t meet all of our objectives, it’s important to pause and acknowledge what we achieved and the important steps we’re taking to rebuild our great business.”

He said 2026 would be “another big year” in its turnaround.

The decision to make the bonus payouts to salaried staff comes as Asda seeks to boost morale after a major shake-up in its top teams.

Last year, staff reported feeling blindsided when Asda scrapped its bonuses for managers. At the time, the supermarket claimed the decision reflected the “challenging year”.

It had sparked warnings from recruiters that Mr Leighton risked “anarchy” in his teams.

However, it followed a torrid year where Asda failed to stem a slump in sales and lost shoppers. Its market share dropped from 13.7pc to 12.6pc between March 2024 and March 2025.

Since then, Asda’s share of the grocery market has declined further to 11.5pc.

The latest industry figures from Worldpanel showed sales were down 2.6pc in the 12 weeks to Feb 22, compared with the same period a year earlier.

Despite the drop, the figures suggest that its performance is starting to improve, with sales having previously been falling as much as 5.8pc on a year on year basis.

Mr Leighton said this month that the Worldpanel data showed that Asda was starting to “edge forward”.

“I’m just looking for signs of progress, and there are signs of progress,” he said.

“But I’ve said from the beginning, this is a [turnaround that will take] three to five years. You do not turn these things around in a short period of time.”

Mr Leighton was brought back at the helm of Asda in 2024 to revive the ailing grocer.

He had previously steered a turnaround of the supermarket in the 1990s and has recently rolled out some of the same initiatives, including a “Tell Allan” recommendation box.

He recently said he had built up a “completely new team” to aid his turnaround drive.

A spokesman for Asda said: “In recognition of all the hard work undertaken during the first year of our turnaround, we have today confirmed that all eligible salaried colleagues will receive a thank you bonus in their March pay. This has been well received.”

data.title: John Lewis to pay first staff bonus in four years
data.description:
John Lewis has brought back its staff bonus for the first time in four years, despite warning over a hit from Rachel Reeves’s tax raids.

The retailer unveiled plans to pay a 2pc bonus to all workers, marking the first time since 2022 it has made an award.

The decision to restore the award follows years in which John Lewis has argued it needed to instead prioritise investment in its stores and in wider pay levels to help fuel a turnaround drive.

Four years ago, staff at both John Lewis and its sister company Waitrose received a 3pc partnership bonus. It employs around 66,000 staff.

The bonus came despite John Lewis missing a profit target it said it would need to hit to pay bonuses.

In its latest annual results, published on Thursday, John Lewis said profits before tax, bonuses and exceptional items came in at £134m in the year to January, up 6pc on £126m a year earlier. It had previously said this would have to reach £200m before it would restore the staff bonus.

It fell to a pre-tax loss of £21m for the year, compared to a £97m profit a year earlier.

However, Jason Tarry, John Lewis’s chairman, said that, although the company missed its target, its “growing cash generation and strong balance sheet enable us to invest more” into its staff and its brands.

He said results had been impacted by “a subdued market, a challenging lead into the crucial peak period and increased taxes”.

It follows a wave of raids on retailers over the past year. John Lewis said it faced a £53m blow from higher National Insurance contributions and new recycling levies.

The company said profit growth was also held back by customers spending “more cautiously, especially in the run up to the peak period”, over Christmas, following a slump in consumer confidence.

John Lewis said it was cautious about trading over the next year, pointing to the ongoing “challenging macroeconomic environment”.

It comes as bosses at the retailer push ahead with a major turnaround drive, having struggled with ballooning losses during the pandemic.

Under Mr Tarry, the company has pledged to refocus on its high street stores, ditching a diversification drive pursued under Dame Sharon White, the former chairman of the John Lewis Partnership.

Dame Sharon had previously set a target to make 40pc of profit from areas outside retail, including housing and financial services, by 2030.

However, the company has since shifted away from that push. Late last month, it announced it was abandoning plans to build 10,000 homes, withdrawing from its build-to-rent business.

data.title: Heathrow boss: Airport isn’t crowded... foreigners just walk the wrong way
data.description:
The chief executive of Heathrow has said the airport is not crowded because foreigners just walk the wrong way.

Thomas Woldbye said people complain the airport is too busy when in fact people are just “crashing into each other”.

This is because European travellers are used to walking on the right, while the British walk on the left.

Mr Woldbye told an Aviation Club UK event on Thursday: “One of the reasons people think [Terminal 5] is crowded – it’s not, actually. But yes, people are in the wrong place.

“Problem is that all the British people keep to the left and normally Europeans keep to the right. And they do that in both directions. So we can be crashing into each other and I see that from personal experience.

“We just [need to] make sure that everybody going this way keeps going left, and [those] going this way keep to the right,” he added, gesturing with his hands to illustrate the different directions.

The humorous aside came after the Danish-born airport boss warned earlier this week that Heathrow could lose its coveted spot as Europe’s busiest airport to rivals such as Amsterdam or Paris unless a third runway is approved for construction.

Mr Woldbye said on Tuesday: “We remain Europe’s largest airport but latest figures show we may lose that position in 2026 and we cannot keep driving growth for the UK economy without more capacity.”

When asked what he thought of the Government’s position on the third runway, he said it was broadly supportive. But he added: “We don’t have long-term regulation, which is clear in terms of us building a business case.”

Last year, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, gave the go-ahead to Heathrow’s preferred plan to build a third runway over the top of the M25 motorway, a scheme likely to generate controversy over its cost and complexity.

The third runway scheme, which will see a 3,500-metre strip built to the north-west of Heathrow’s existing site, is currently estimated to cost around £49bn.

Included in the controversial plans are a scheme to build a 66,000-space car park at a cost of £2.6bn, as revealed by the City AM financial newspaper last summer.

The airport boss was also criticised on Thursday for raising drop-off fees paid by drivers to £7 – and threatening an £80 fine for motorists spending longer than 10 minutes saying goodbye to their loved ones.

He said in response to the criticism: “So when we make profits on all parts of the business, rather than just running the airport, that actually goes to reducing the charge [applied to passengers’ tickets].

“Whenever we make less money ... passengers are actually paying [for] that,” he added.

“We have an incentive to make as much money as we can, on the side, from anything that actually reduces the charge for passengers.”

Heathrow’s charges are regulated by the Government. The airport pays for its infrastructure by imposing a charge on airline passengers’ tickets.

Airlines, including Virgin Atlantic and British Airways, have set up a pressure group called Heathrow Reimagined, which calls for “urgent and fundamental reform” of this funding system.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘A nest of Left-wing vipers’: 1986 was the BBC’s first annus horribilis
data.description:
In 2026, we are used to a BBC which is constantly mired in scandal, from allegations of editorial bias on Panorama to questions over severe lapses of judgment at last year’s Glastonbury. However, 40 years ago, the corporation was also staggering from controversy to controversy. Margaret Thatcher, the prime minister at the time, believed it was (in the evocative words of Michael Grade, then Controller of BBC One) “a nest of Left-wing vipers”.

The problems started in 1985 when the Troubles in Northern Ireland were all-consuming. A documentary, Real Lives: At the Edge of the Union, featured an interview with Martin McGuinness, then one of the leaders of the Irish republican party Sinn Féin.

Grade describes it to me thus: “I passed it for transmission, and then a Sunday Times journalist asked Mrs Thatcher on a Washington trip, ‘Why are you broadcasting a documentary giving airtime to terrorists?’ She was horrified and said, ‘We are not in the business of giving terrorists the oxygen of publicity.’”

The battle lines were swiftly drawn between the government and the BBC. With Thatcher at the height of her power, it seemed as if there could only be one winner. Grade says “The board of governors, then led by William Rees-Mogg, went ballistic, and they decided, stupidly, to review the programme. Then they announced that it wasn’t fit for transmission, and banned it.” However, Grade and his colleagues refused to take this decision lying down. “At this point we all went out on the streets to protest the decision – the only time I’ve ever demonstrated – so the programme was transmitted, with a few minor changes.” BBC one, government nil.

Today, Grade places the blame squarely at the hands of the BBC’s board of governors. “The decision for the board to act as the editorial guardians of the BBC’s output was preposterous, and a terrible mistake, but it was the director-general Alasdair Milne’s call, not [chairman of the board] Stuart Young’s fault. The board was dominated by yes-people Mrs Thatcher had put in place.” However, it sounds as if Grade – who eventually became a Conservative peer in 2011 – relished the fight. “Thatcher was easy to wind up, because she thought of us [the BBC] as dominated by the Left-wing.”

The Falklands Play

The fight had implications for any kind of political drama, too, which came about when Ian Curteis’s television film, The Falklands Play, ran into trouble. It had been commissioned by Milne, rather than the head of drama, in 1982, just after the conclusion of the Falklands War, without a script.

Grade recalls: “When the script did arrive, it wasn’t very good.” Curteis proved to be a less than amicable collaborator. “Every time we went back to him and asked him for some rewrites, he immediately twisted it and said, ‘The BBC is trying to stop me writing what I believe is Thatcher’s finest hour.’ Eventually I rang him and said, ‘Ian, I’ve read the script and you have to work with the drama department.’

“I told him, ‘You haven’t made clear what you, as the author, think about the Belgrano. You should have a point of view in the script.’ He twisted that and said that I was trying to do an anti-Thatcher script that was attacking her for the sinking of the Belgrano.”

Tensions rose, in part, because Grade was a great admirer of Charles Wood’s script for another, more nuanced film about the Falklands, called Tumbledown. It would eventually be filmed by Richard Eyre and star a pre-fame Colin Firth as the British Army officer Robert Lawrence, who was shot in the head by a sniper during the conflict and left with horrendous wounds including paralysis. It was the opposite of The Falklands Play: cynical, timely and decidedly political. Unfortunately, the BBC board decided that it had to run Curteis’s drama.

Grade tells me now: “We decided that the Curteis script wasn’t good enough, but I was being told that we had to do The Falklands Play. It was my judgment that we shouldn’t spend a million quid of the licence-fee-payers’ money on poor writing, and that the author had politicised the changes that we wanted to make. But we were told that if we wanted to do Tumbledown, we’d have to do The Falklands Play too. We wanted to do a play about Captain Lawrence, and how the military establishment didn’t want people to know how horrendous the consequences of war are. Then I found out that we were being accused of being Left-wing and anti-war.”

While Grade now suggests: “I was prepared to resign over The Falklands Play, although the board didn’t know that,” they eventually relented and the BBC was able to make Tumbledown, which was aired in 1988 to an appreciative audience of 10 million. The Falklands Play was eventually produced in 2002, starring Patricia Hodge as Mrs Thatcher, with the script including the cuts that Curteis didn’t want to make.

The Monocled Mutineer

The sense of the BBC being dangerously subversive continued when Alan Bleasdale’s controversial drama The Monocled Mutineer was aired in August 1986. Adapted from the 1978 non-fiction book of the same name by William Allison and John Fairley, it explored a British mutiny at a French training camp in Étaples during the First World War. Paul McGann played Percy Toplis, a private who led the mutiny while disguised as an officer. According to both the book and screenplay, Toplis was later hunted down by Special Branch and killed as a punishment for his role in inciting revolt, which took place as a result of the brutal and inhumane treatment that the men received.

The reason for the show’s controversy was twofold. First, it had been government policy ever to deny that there had been a mutiny at Étaples, or that its ringleaders had been executed for their part in it. (All the relevant papers had long since been destroyed.) Second, it was believed that this was yet another example of Left-wing bias, of fictionalising the incidents of 1917 for agitprop purposes.

John Keegan, The Telegraph’s defence correspondent at the time, pooh-poohed the whole basis on which the four-part series was made, saying “The Étaples ‘mutinies’ amounted to no more than a few days of disorder, a little disrespect to officers and some loudly voiced demands for humane treatment. The Army reacted briskly. It restored discipline by bringing in unaffected troops. It removed the cause of discontent by replacing the worst of the staff with wise men. That is about all there was to the British Army ‘mutinies’ of the 1914-1918 war.”

Others disagreed, although Julian Putkowski, the show’s historical consultant, described it as “riddled with error” and called it “a sensational version of the mutiny and Toplis’s life”. The real-life Toplis was not the dashing, if anti-heroic, figure portrayed by McGann but a man convicted of attempted rape and fraud who was eventually shot by the police while on the run after he murdered a taxi driver.

Nevertheless, Grade says: “After transmission, I got half a dozen letters in shaky handwriting from veterans saying ‘It’s all true, this is what happened.’”

Casualty

If The Monocled Mutineer was always going to prove controversial, the same could not be said of the BBC’s new medical drama that launched on September 6 1986. Casualty is one of the biggest hits in British television history, and is now the longest-running series of its kind in the world; a populist show which is an irresistible mix of soapy relationships and edge-of-the seat emergencies. Back then, however, the fledgling series got it in the jugular from Edwina Currie, then-junior health secretary, who denounced it in the House of Commons as “anti-NHS propaganda” and “Left-wing rubbish” that portrayed the health service – the envy of the world, lest we forget – as a flawed, often bureaucratic institution that was barely able to cope with the horrors that befell its patients.

Today, Currie still stands by some of her criticisms. As she tells me, “My concern was that Casualty was relentlessly negative, with the staff always moaning – it appeared to have a strong anti-government agenda. Yet we had a big hospitals building programme, we had remarkable health promotion programmes under way, including anti-smoking campaigns (which worked), the introduction of revolutionary breast cancer screening and cervical cancer screening, we were battling Aids/HIV with a widely respected programme, and as a result for the first time ever the UK was spending more on health than defence.”

However, Currie also had a (perhaps inadvertently) positive effect on the show’s future. “I’d recently pointed out that EastEnders wasn’t helping when all the action was in a pub. That worked as they introduced the allotments, and started putting bowls of fruit on the tables for domestic scenes.”

The Troubles, war, mutiny, the NHS in crisis: 1986 highlights that, in some ways, nothing ever changes. But Grade tells me that, “Everything that we wanted to get made, we got made”. The BBC was able to weather the storm. Today, it might be a different story.
