data.title: Terrible reviews for my new book have made me a better person
data.description:
I knew that my new book about female freedom would ignite disagreement. Good Slut, its title, is triggering for a good many. But molten contempt?

The Lefty female reviewerati are a feisty lot. Having kept somewhat to the side of the fiery bear pit of feminism for the last few years, I was perhaps ill-prepared for finding myself, even briefly, in its bloodbath.

But I was still knocked sideways by the sheer nastiness of the first review, by Sarah Ditum in The Spectator. Part of the trenchant “gender critical” crew, Ditum is one of those women whose knee-jerk reaction as a “feminist” is to fall right into the cliché of catfight, going straight for the low blows, and the jugular, when she doesn’t like an argument or, God forbid, a book.

We’re on the same team really. But then off she goes, shooting from what struck me as a rather Freudian hip, lambasting my “scattershot” ideas, “endemically sloppy” text, alleged errors (the example given was her error, actually), and my apparent incontinence as both a writer and thinker. She might as well have called me a hysteric. She certainly made no concession to the possibility I did know what I was writing about, given that I wrote an Mphil, PhD and an academic tome (given five stars, no less, by The Telegraph’s Tim Stanley) in this area.

She called me “intellectually dishonest and politically unserious” and concluded, “if this is the best defence that the sexual revolution can muster, the future looks dry indeed.”

To get this kind of vengeful walloping felt shocking, and not nice at all, especially as someone who – for all that I write about controversial matters – really detests personal conflict.

I don’t expect good reviews or agreement. But to feel the fury of girl-on-girl nastiness is rather shocking if you’ve generally stayed out of it. Still, through the nasty feelings, reminiscent of being back in the schoolyard, I felt interest: Ditum appeared to be doing what men have done for time immemorial, namely erasing and trampling on a woman’s attainments by making her out to be a mad illogical fool because they don’t agree with, or feel threatened, by what she is saying.

As the days trickled by, I rallied. I began to see that I’d poked a bear that hates being poked. Instead of worrying that perhaps I really am a terrible fraud, I remembered that other readers enthused and did not find the book “dishonest”, “lazy”, “sloppy” or “fatuous”.

I knew there was a discontented review in The Observer, but I gave it a wide berth once I’d determined it wasn’t a hatchet job. A glowing review came out in The Telegraph from the estimable free-thinker Ella Whelan. The largely enjoyable demands of motherhood took precedence.

But, sitting in the Green Room at Jewish Book Week, after a lovely long discussion with Michael Gove, my eye was caught by a post on X by one of those Bright Young Men of journalism, whom I considered a colleague and acquaintance. Above another vicious, somewhat messier karate chop in the New Statesman, the wunderkind snarled that my book looked absolutely “risible”. Good lord! What had I ever done to the magazine, apart from write for it and engage politely with its editors at parties?

A battle-worn matriarch sharing the Green Room told me, with love, to “toughen up”. I vowed to do so.

I’ve learnt a few things this week. One is that I do need to toughen up. The other is that when you write a book with bold ideas you soon discover that in journalism, you really do have no idea who your friends are and who can’t wait to stab you full frontally.

‘Good Slut: How Money, Sex and Power Set Women Free’ by Zoe Strimpel is out now

data.title: Bank of England redundancy scheme overwhelmed as 700 ask for pay out
data.description:
More than 700 staff at the Bank of England applied to leave their roles in return for a pay-out as it experienced overwhelming demand for a voluntary redundancy scheme.

The Bank said 446 people will leave their roles – equal to around 8pc of its employees – after they came forward to take part in the scheme.

According to Financial News, which first reported the news, 712 staff applied for the scheme, suggesting that there was significantly more demand than the Bank of England was able to meet.

The Bank will pay out a total of £36m in settlements, meaning the 446 employees leaving will receive an average of £81,000 each.

The departures come as the Bank of England races to find savings to fund an overhaul of its flawed economic forecasts. It follows recommendations made by Ben Bernanke, the Federal Reserve chair, in a report in 2024.

The report called for the Bank to overhaul its forecasting and communications infrastructure, which has led to squeezed budgets elsewhere in the organisation.

The Bank is aiming to save 8pc of its operating costs during the financial year – equivalent to around £45m – and has already decided to shelve investments considered non-essential.

It is understood that the scheme will account for £35m of those savings, leaving a further £10m of savings to be found elsewhere.

The Bank of England had not been targeting a specific number of staff to depart as part of the redundancy scheme.

However, Andrew Bailey, the Bank of England Governor, warned that further job cuts could come on top of the “entirely voluntary” scheme. Speaking in December, he said: “We cannot rule out compulsory redundancies later down the line.”

Mr Bernanke’s report previously called on the Bank of England to consider publishing its own outlook for UK interest rates.

While it noted that the Bank’s forecasts during the recent inflation shock was no worse than other banks, it warned that the infrastructure that underpins its projections was in need of an urgent upgrade.

The software and models used to produce its forecasts were “out of date” and “not adequately maintained”, Mr Bernanke said at the time. He added that “makeshift fixes” had resulted in an unwieldy and inflexible system, limiting the ability of staff to conduct useful analysis.

A Bank of England spokesman said: “The Bank is currently implementing a significant, multi-year transformation of our operations ensuring we are efficient, resilient, fit for the future and stick to our budget.

“As a result of the mutually agreed, time-limited resignation scheme launched last year, just under 450 staff will depart the Bank in the coming months.”

The spokesman added: “This scheme leaves the Bank in a stronger position to deliver the savings we need to make to help us deliver on our statutory objectives to maintain monetary and financial stability.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Goldman banker: Clients ‘glad’ for ‘distraction’ of Iran war
data.description:
A senior Goldman Sachs banker has reportedly said some clients were “glad” the Iran war is providing a “distraction” from concerns about AI and private credit.

Kunal Shah, the co-chief executive of Goldman Sachs International, said on a call with clients some people were happy attention was being moved away from fears about a meltdown in private credit and concerns AI would destroy many business models.

Some clients are “just glad there’s something to talk about that isn’t software exposures and private credit”, he said, according to the Financial Times, “and this is at least a distraction from that”.

Fears that technology companies might be vulnerable to disruption from AI have led to ructions in the private credit market, which has lent heavily to software businesses.

Shares in listed private credit firms have tumbled, with tech-focused lender Blue Owl Capital down almost 40pc this year. It restricted cash withdrawals at one of its funds last month after a flood of withdrawal requests.

A spokesman for Goldman Sachs told the Financial Times Mr Shah was “asked a question about what he was hearing from clients and shared his observations from multiple points of view”. 

It came as the boss of Legal & General said the pensions giant was “very comfortable” with its own exposures to private credit.

More than a fifth of Legal & General’s diversified annuity portfolio, which backs the company’s annuity liabilities and generates the returns needed to pay policyholders, is invested in private credit.

Concerns about the market, often called “shadow banking”, have ramped up in recent months after a series of high-profile failures, including UK-based Market Financial Solutions.

“It’s a very hot area in the market with lots of interest, but private credit, specifically in our own exposure, we’re very comfortable with,” António Simões, Legal & General’s chief, said.

Andrew Kail, the pension giant’s chief financial officer, added that the company was “very selective about where we invest”.

“Partly because of our plans and partly because as an insurance company, there are only certain types of assets that we are able to invest in,” he added.

Ructions in the private credit market have been focused on direct lending to the technology sector, which is under threat from advancements in AI.

However, the majority of Legal & General’s investments within private credit are focused on areas such as infrastructure, utilities and property.

The comments came as Legal & General published its full-year results, where it reported an increase in private market assets under management of almost a third to £75bn.

Mr Simões added that the company was “well on track” to beat its target of having £85bn assets under management in the sector by 2028. A significant part of Legal & General’s investment in the private markets are its stakes in the build-to-rent sector.

Legal & General also unveiled a £1.2bn share buyback, its largest ever. However, its shares slid more than 6pc.

The company reported a 6pc increase in core operating profit, slightly less than market expectations. The group’s solvency cover ratio – a key metric of financial strength – also dropped to 210pc from 232pc, which analysts at RBC Capital said was a “significant miss” compared to expectations.

Goldman Sachs was contacted for comment.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Dull purchases can save our High Streets
data.description:
In 1938 James Maude Richards, the newly appointed editor of The Architectural Review, published High Street, a survey of the distinctive shops of the British high street, beautifully illustrated by Eric Ravilious.

Almost a century on, many of the shops described by Richards have vanished: the plumassier and submarine engineer are extinct while the knife grinder and model ship maker are on the red list.

Those of us lucky enough to live in a town with a still-thriving high street cherish the latter-day incarnations of Richards’s family butcher, pharmacy and hardware shop. But in his introduction, he sounded the alarm: “In many places the personal and local character of these shops is disappearing… many shops are now only branches of the big multiple stores.”

Defying his gloomy predictions, the traditional high street survived into the mid-20th century, until forward-looking local authorities ripped the heart out of provincial towns, demolishing modest vernacular architecture to build brutalist shopping centres, which were eclipsed in their turn by out-of-town superstores, becoming shuttered rookeries for disaffected teens.

By the early 21st century, politicians had belatedly become alarmed by the death of the high street. In 2012, David Cameron commissioned a “Save the High Street” campaign headed by the “Queen of Shops”, Mary Portas. Five years later, The Telegraph reported that the twelve towns targeted by the campaign had lost almost 1,000 shops between them.

Now here we are again, trapped in retail Groundhog Day (but worse, thanks to Covid, online shopping and the recent changes to business rates). In Westminster, the bitter resentment at the death of their town centres by an electorate not prepared to relinquish their online and out-of-town shopping habits must seem inexplicable.

But the explanation will come at the ballot box, and so we have the launch of the £5bn “pride in place” regeneration scheme, announced in January by Steve Reed, the Housing, Communities and Local Government Secretary, and the forthcoming “town of culture” scheme announced by Lisa Nandy. The trouble with terms such as “regeneration” and “culture” is that to citizens trudging past the vape shops, slots arcades and derelict shopfronts of their local high street, they sound as flimsy as the fast-food debris littering their pavements.

In The Telegraph last week, Ben Marlow argued that “soulless” shops – the “big multiples” regretted by James Maude Richards – could be the saviours of our high streets, acting as “anchors” to attract shoppers. If the duller bits of life admin – picking up a prescription, a pint of milk and some batteries – were frictionless and could be combined with a small reward – coffee with a friend, or some window-shopping – who wouldn’t prefer that to driving out of town, or waiting in for an online delivery?

The high street of my local town tends to prove the point. Anchored by a “soulless” pair of supermarkets (with convenient car parks), it has a pharmacy, a trio of mid-range clothes shops, a couple of banks, a library, small post office and a public loo. People visit for the dull stuff and linger for the coffee and the browsing.

A visiting friend said the ironmongers was his idea of heaven: they sell posh paint, mousetraps and little brushes for cleaning mushrooms – and if you need your shears sharpened, they’ll do that too. Steve Reed and Lisa Nandy should visit some time.

data.title: Royal Mail to deliver 219 million letters late this year
data.description:
Royal Mail will deliver 219 million letters late this year, even after the postal service raised stamp prices.

The company has insisted its quality of service is improving, with more than 92pc of all letters arriving on or before their due delivery date this year so far.

But MPs have accused the company of obscuring the true scale of delays by grouping first-class and second-class post together.

In fact, less than 75pc of first-class mail has been delivered the next working day – well below targets of 93pc. That equates to 126 million first-class letters arriving later than their scheduled arrival date during the year.

Second-class post has performed better, with more than 90pc delivered on time, though this remains well behind target and still translates to around 93 million late letters. Overall, Royal Mail is on track to deliver 219 million letters late.

Despite the poor quality of service, Royal Mail has announced it will increase the price of a first-class stamp by 10p to £1.80 next month.

It marks the seventh price rise in four years and means the price of a first class stamp has almost doubled since 2022, when it cost just 95p. Second-class stamp prices, which are capped by regulator Ofcom, will increase from 87p to 91p.

Royal Mail, which is owned by Daniel Křetínský, a Czech billionaire, is under fierce scrutiny from politicians about its quality of service following widespread reports of late or missing letters.

MPs on the business and trade committee last month launched an investigation into the issue, raising concerns that the postal service was prioritising its lucrative parcels business instead of letters.

In its response, Royal Mail said it did not have specific data on when parcels were prioritised over letters.

It said its letter-sorting technology was designed to group letters together for delivery on a specific date but denied it would hold back mail if that meant it would miss its target delivery date.

However, MPs pointed out that Royal Mail had repeatedly fallen short of its delivery targets. Ofcom hit the company with a record £21m fine in October, taking its total penalties over the last three years to more than £36m.

Royal Mail is attempting to thrash out the details of reforms to its universal service obligation, which were approved by Ofcom last year.

This will allow the postal service to scrap second-class letter deliveries, while it will also water down its delivery targets to reflect a sharp decline in letter sending.

But the reforms have been delayed by a standoff with Royal Mail’s powerful postal union, which argues that trials that began more than a year ago are not working.

The two sides are now locked in intense negotiations overseen by ministers, which have been extended from its original four-week deadline. Royal Mail has said it will push ahead with its reforms if no agreement is reached by the end of the talks.

Royal Mail bosses are set to appear before MPs later this month alongside officials from the Communication Workers Union and Ofcom.

While Royal Mail acknowledged that the quality of service could be improved but insisted that delivery times had improved and that 5.8 billion letters arrived on time each year.

It added that almost half of all letters are now sent via a cheaper five-day service offered to bulk senders such as the NHS, banks and energy companies.

A Royal Mail spokesman said: “We recognise there is more to do to deliver a consistently high level of service everywhere.

“Attending the business and trade committee will give us the opportunity to discuss the work we are doing to transform Royal Mail and the urgent need to implement changes to the universal service to deliver the services our customers want and ensure we are financially sustainable for the long term.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Millions of households face higher water bills to meet rising debt costs
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher water bills after five of Britain’s biggest suppliers were allowed to raise charges to help fund debt costs.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said it had decided to allow Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water to increase customers’ bills by an extra 2.2pc on average.

This is on top of previous bill increases allowed by Ofwat. The water regulator already approved a 24pc increase to annual water bills by the five utility firms between late 2024 and 2030, with the firms having appealed to be allowed to push through steeper rises.

The CMA’s approval will mean even sharper increases in water bills for 14 million people.

Some households are facing steeper rises than others. South East Water, for example, has been allowed to raise bills by an extra 4pc to £284 a year by 2030, while Anglian Water can increase them by an additional 2pc to £602.

The CMA said the increases reflected “the higher cost of financing which makes investment more costly to secure for water companies compared to Ofwat’s price review”.

Water companies in England and Wales are struggling with heavy debts.

South East Water’s operating company has a debt pile of £1.3bn, which is more than four times its annual revenue. Anglian Water’s debt surpassed £7.7bn at its last annual results.

Southern Water’s owner pumped hundreds of millions of pounds into the utility firm last July to see off a debt crunch.

The utility firms have argued that they need to be able to secure extra investment to “meet their environmental and drinking water quality legal obligations” and to help boost investor returns “to reflect the risks involved”.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said it had “allowed limited extra funding where that’s genuinely needed, balancing concerns about affordability with the need to secure our water supplies and cut pollution”.

‘These people should be in jail’

It follows mounting criticism from customers over the service water companies provide, as well as their pollution records.

In December, 24,000 homes served by South East Water in Tunbridge Wells were left without drinkable water for nearly two weeks.

Southern Water, which also argued that it needed to raise bills, recorded 269 pollution incidents in 2024. In 2021, the company was hit with a £90m fine for dumping billions of litres of raw sewage into protected seas.

Feargal Sharkey, a clean water campaigner, said: “What’s clear is that every household in the country is simply being abused and extorted by the greatest crime gang in history, the water industry and our government does nothing.”

Mr Sharkey added: “These people should be in jail. They should not be allowed to fleece customers even further.”

Since 1989, when the water companies were privatised, they have paid out £78bn in dividends.

A spokesman for South East Water said: “The decision to appeal was a difficult one, but one we took in the interests of our customers to ensure we were appropriately funded to deliver the infrastructure required to tackle the challenges faced in the South East.”

Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water were contacted for comment.

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: ‘My £199 robot mower just spins around in circles and Lidl won’t fix it’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Our consumer champion, Ruth Emery, is here to help. For how to contact Ruth, click here*.

Dear Ruth,

My wife purchased a Parkside robot mower from our local Lidl store for £199 in March 2025. This item came with a battery charger, perimeter wire and other items to allow autonomous operation – ideal for our small garden.

We liked that the mower could be programmed to mow the lawn independently from its charging station at preset times. But we didn’t get to use this self-start feature.

While all was great for three or four uses, the machine then lost all settings other than “spot mowing”. This set the lawn mower into a spiral cutting motion and the only way to get it to operate normally was to nudge the front.

Initially we went back to the Lidl store who advised us to contact Parkside directly and go through the warranty procedure. Parkside promised us a new unit but it has not yet appeared.

I’ve been waiting – and chasing – for nine months.

Please can you help?

– SL, Lancashire

Dear Reader,

Your robot mower refuses to do anything apart from move in circles – and you’ve also been going round and round in circles trying to get the issue fixed.

Parkside is a Lidl brand for DIY and garden products. You contacted Parkside’s customer service, which is handled by the after-sales service provider Novo CSV. You were advised last May to complete a factory reset and if that didn’t work, to try a hard reset by removing the battery for 24 hours.

Neither worked. You noted that when the mower started in “spot mode”, if it encountered an obstacle, such as a heavy plant pot, it appeared to reset and operate normally.

The reader’s robot mower operating normally before it later malfunctioned.

But, of course, you had bought the item so it could happily trundle around your garden at pre-set times, resulting in a smartly mown lawn. This should happen automatically and you shouldn’t have to hit it or hope it bumps into something for it to work properly.

You were then told in August that a replacement part was needed and would be shipped to you.

There was no sign of anything by October and after sending several follow-up emails, you were informed the parts were coming from the manufacturer in Germany and should arrive by mid-December.

Fast forward to 2026 and you’re still waiting.

I asked Lidl to liaise with Novo CSV and sort this out for you.

After investigating for a few days, Lidl said there were delays getting the replacement – and it didn’t know when the item would be back in stock – so it would give you a refund if you returned the mower in-store, plus a £50 voucher.

A spokesman said: “We work closely with our suppliers to ensure the products we sell are of high quality and that customers receive a high level of service. We are disappointed that our standards were not met on this occasion and would like to apologise for the inconvenience caused by offering a full refund and a store voucher to the customer.”

You’re pleased the “saga has been brought to a satisfactory conclusion”.

It seems other customers aren’t impressed with their Parkside robot mowers, with complaints on Trustpilot ranging from charging problems to mowers digging holes in the grass and getting stuck.

I imagine this bargain £199 item was in the discount supermarket’s famous “middle aisle”, where customers are often tempted to impulsively buy random items such as fishing rods, home gym equipment or ride-on toy cars at ultra-low prices.

Unfortunately, while there’s no denying the budget-friendly price tags, the quality can be lacking – as you’ve found out.

Consumer Champion Katie Morley is currently on maternity leave. Read her previous investigations here.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Boss of collapsed £2bn ‘shadow bank’ accused of stealing money from lender
data.description:
The boss of a collapsed £2bn shadow bank has been accused of using a network of fake companies to steal money from the company’s creditors.

Paresh Raja, the founder of Market Financial Solutions (MFS) is alleged to have used eight companies to extract money from creditors on false pretences, a legal filing from a creditor group alleges.

The creditors claimed that while the eight were purported by MFS to be “genuine borrowers” they were in fact “closely connected with” MFS.

Creditors to MFS successfully placed these eight companies into administration on Tuesday.

Six of the eight are owned by two individuals who are connected to Magus Chartered Accountancy, a small London-based firm that appears to play a central role in the MFS saga.

On Sunday, The Telegraph revealed that Magus acted as accountants for MFS while also appearing to serve as the owners of a large number of companies that received the MFS loans.

MFS was placed into administration late last month amid what a judge called “very serious” allegations of fraud.

As a so-called shadow bank, MFS did not take deposits and instead funded its loans by borrowing from itself.

A cast of high-profile banks – including Santander, Wells Fargo, Jefferies and Barclays – backed MFS.

‘Fraudulent wrongdoing’

Its demise not only shocked the City, but has prompted the Bank of England to investigate the circumstances of the collapse.

On Wednesday, another company owned by a Magus director was successfully placed into administration based on that it appeared to have been controlled by Mr Raja himself and was used to “perpetrate fraudulent wrongdoing”, according to legal filings seen by Bloomberg.

More than 130 companies linked to Magus have received loans from MFS, according to a Telegraph analysis of Companies House filings, which could represent up to a fifth of MFS’s total clients.

Of the companies controlled by Magus, more than half received loans from different MFS lenders on the same day, secured by the same property.

In their claim, the creditors warned that lending to connected borrowers, as well as the “double-pledging” of collateral to secure multiple loans, has led to a hole in MFS’s accounts of more than £1.3bn.

Established in 2002, Magus’s own accounts show it had just two employees.

Mike Stubbs, a partner at Mishcon de Reya, legal counsel to Paresh Raja, said: “Mistakes have been made but there has been no intention to defraud whatsoever and Mr Raja has not been the beneficiary of any shortfall (if any) there may be.”

“These allegations are based on fundamental misunderstandings and assumptions and are materially incorrect,” Mr Stubbs added.

“Both Mr Raja and Mr Hurhangee will fully and happily assist the liquidators of Twinwin, indeed are already doing so.”

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: Fuel tankers divert to Asia in threat to British supplies
data.description:
Tankers laden with liquefied natural gas (LNG) are being rerouted from Europe to Asia as the Iran energy crisis forces countries to bid against each other for supplies.

A string of mostly American vessels that were previously headed for the Continent have changed course at sea to chase bigger paydays in Japan, Korea and China, analysts said.

Eight vessels have already been diverted, while more than 10 others are also considered candidates for diversion, according to the commodities data company Kpler.

It means gas prices in Britain and across Europe will have to rise above those in Asia to secure supplies, with the increases passed on to households and businesses through their bills.

Gas prices have already rocketed after Iran threatened to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz – the choke point through which one fifth of global LNG supplies pass – triggering a near-total halt to traffic in and out of the Persian Gulf.

It has forced Qatar, the world’s second-largest exporter of LNG, to stop exports and declare force majeure.

Asian countries that usually depend on Qatar’s supplies – including China, India, Japan and South Korea – have been left scrambling for alternatives.

As of Monday morning, the spot price for gas in Japan and Korea was about €66 (£57) per megawatt hour, compared with €62 per megawatt hour in Europe, according to an analysis by Panmure Liberum.

While the Asian price has been broadly flat since Friday, the European price has jumped from €53 per megawatt hour.

Laura Page, an LNG expert at Kpler, said gas prices were expected to rise even further amid competition for cargoes.

She said most of the ships that were changing course had set out from the US, but some were vessels that had started in Nigeria, adding: “It’s not anything unusual to see diversions happening, but in this case, the scale is quite significant.

“The first cargo that we saw was last week. It likely started to divert on March 3 and was a vessel that loaded in Nigeria and was due to head to France.”

“But then what we see is that they essentially stop and turn around and start heading to Asia.”

She said Kpler had “seen a total of eight cargoes doing that kind of diversion activity”, adding: “Most are US cargoes.”

One vessel that diverted last week was the Simsimah, according to MarineTraffic, a ship-tracking website.

The ship was loaded with an American-produced cargo of LNG at Plaquemines, New Orleans, and then began sailing across the Atlantic.

At the start of last week, its declared destination was still Europe. However, on Thursday, it had changed to Asia, and on Monday this week, it was seen sailing south off the coast of West Africa.

Ms Page said there was now a question of whether Asia would “continue bidding up the market and pricing for more supply to head that way, or is Europe going to start competing?”

‘Major price hike for consumers’

Although the heating season is nearing its end on the Continent, storage levels across the European Union have dipped below 30pc, compared to 37pc a year ago.

It has prompted concerns that stocks will need to be rebuilt to a greater degree ahead of next winter.

Countries such as Italy and Poland, which have Qatari supply contracts, will also seek to replace what they have lost, forcing European and Asian buyers into further competition.

Ms Page added: “That absolutely has implications for our household bills. The key question is, how long is this going to last?

“If it’s just a short thing, then probably prices will come down pretty quickly. But the general feeling in the market is this is starting to rumble on, and for every day that this continues, we’re losing 20pc of the world’s LNG supply.

“When those weeks add up into months, this is going to translate into a major price hike for consumers.”

An analysis by Stifel last week predicted that the UK’s energy price cap could hit £2,500 per year if the Iran crisis dragged on for more than a month and pushed up gas prices. The cap currently stands at £1,758 per year.

Reports on Monday suggested the Government was mulling a potential support package for households. However, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, told MPs it was “too early to tell the impact” of the crisis.

She said: “Of course, we will take the necessary actions to protect consumers and businesses.

“But the most important thing we can do at the moment is de-escalate the conflict and work with [marine insurer] Lloyd’s of London and with countries around the world to get those vessels flowing through the Strait of Hormuz.

“That is what is absolutely key for containing the rises in energy prices on defence spending.”

data.title: Borrowing costs rise at fastest pace since Truss mini-Budget
data.description:
Britain’s borrowing costs are rising at their fastest pace since the Liz Truss mini-Budget crisis, as the war in Iran sends shockwaves through markets.

The yield on 10-year UK gilts, as the government bonds are known, has risen by more than 0.4 percentage points this week from 4.23pc to 4.69pc.

It marks the sharpest increase in the yield on the bonds – considered a benchmark for the cost of servicing Britain’s £2.9tn national debt – in four years.

The last time borrowing costs rose this quickly was the week after Truss’s chancellor Kwasi Kwarteng unveiled £45bn of unfunded tax cuts in his mini-Budget in September 2022. Then, the yield on 10-year gilts jumped by 54 basis points in a week.

If sustained, the latest increase in Government borrowing costs will wipe £1.5bn off the Chancellor’s fiscal headroom, according to Andrew Goodwin, an economist at Oxford Economics.

Bond yields – the return the Treasury pays to buyers of its debt – have surged at a breakneck pace around the world over fears that soaring oil and gas prices will trigger a resurgence in inflation.

James Athey, a fund manager at Marlborough Group, said rising oil and gas prices had put “energy-sensitive economies like Europe and the UK in the firing line”.

He said: “Investors don’t know how long this disruption will last and ultimately how central banks will respond – in the absence of certainty, it’s a sensible option to reduce the likelihood of near-term rate cuts.”

Mr Athey said bond yields have also surged as hedge funds struggle to unwind large bets that interest rates would fall over the course of this year. This had caused “chunky moves in shorter dated yields”, he said. The yield on two-year UK gilts has surged by more than 40 basis points this week, in the biggest jump since August 2024.

Mr Athey said: “It’s not a rational repricing, and it’s taken UK yields to a level which is far too high in our opinion, but in the short term it is difficult to stand in the way amidst so much uncertainty and ahead of a weekend.”

The war in Iran has seen dramatic movements in markets as investors try to gauge the impact of a potentially long period of disruption to oil and gas supplies.

Traders have effectively ruled out another rate cut by the Bank of England later this year, having priced in at least two reductions just a week ago.

Oxford Economics predicted that household energy bills would rise by 13.5pc from July as the effect of higher oil and gas prices filters through to the UK economy.

Edward Allenby, a senior economist, said he expects shipments through the Strait of Hormuz to be disrupted for at least two months, pushing UK inflation this year from an average of 2.3pc to 2.7pc as oil prices average just under $80 (£59.70) a barrel in the second quarter.

But Neil Birrell, at Premier Miton Investors, warned that “even now the much-heightened risks from the war in the Middle East are only getting reflected in commodity and rates markets”.

He said: “The speed and scale of those moves must impact equities and other assets soon.”

Brent, the international benchmark, surpassed $90 on Friday for the first time in two years, putting it on track for its biggest weekly gain since the first Covid lockdown in 2020.

European gas prices have surged by 67pc over the past week, their steepest rise since Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

Robin Brooks, a former chief economist at the Institute of International Finance, said the risks from this conflict are “an order of magnitude bigger” for oil markets than Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

He said he was more pessimistic because the Iranian regime “is battling for its survival”.

data.title: Watchdog censures Telegraph over ‘foreign benefits’ story – but accepts it was true
data.description:
The press watchdog has been criticised for a “bizarre” decision to censure The Telegraph over a story it accepts is true.

The Independent Press Standards Organisation (Ipso) upheld a complaint over the accuracy of an article headlined: “More than one million foreign nationals claiming benefits”, saying the information the article was based on did not prove the central claim.

In fact, figures from the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) show that the article, published in March last year, underestimated the number of foreign nationals in receipt of benefits, which was actually 1.2 million.

Nevertheless, The Telegraph has been told to print a correction online and in print, which includes Ipso’s ruling questioning the way the figures were presented.

Ipso stated that because the DWP data on which the article was based recorded the nationality of claimants when registering for a National Insurance number, rather than the nationality at the time they were claiming benefits, some claimants might have since acquired UK citizenship, and would not be foreign nationals at the time they were claiming benefits, but that the article did not make this clear.

Ipso agreed, however, that The Telegraph could also publish alongside its ruling the fact that DWP figures released more recently show that more than 1.2 million people with a non-British-citizen immigration status were indeed claiming Universal Credit at the time in question, and that the figure had been above one million since July 2023.

‘Bizarre decision’

Sir David Davis, the Conservative MP and free speech campaigner, said: “This seems to me to be an extraordinarily unwise decision by Ipso.

“I really do think Ipso should look back at their own decision-making. This is not the only time we have had some odd decisions from them.”

Zia Yusuf, Reform UK’s spokesman for home affairs, called it a “bizarre decision” by Ipso.

He said: “Britain’s press watchdog has forced a newspaper to print a correction about a migration story they themselves accept is true.

“It looks like an attempt to massage the narrative and downplay the true scale of Britain’s migration crisis.”

The Telegraph’s story, published in March 2025, was based on an analysis of DWP data by the Centre for Migration Control (CMC).

‘Beyond question’ report was correct

Robert Bates, the research director at the CMC, said: “The modelling published by The Telegraph – which showed over one million foreigners were claiming benefits in March 2025 – was subsequently corroborated by an official DWP data release. It is beyond question that the reporting was correct.

“We are proud that our estimates accurately raised this huge problem to the attention of the British public, and the reporting no doubt played an important role in persuading the Government to finally publish this important data in full.”

It is not the first time a ruling by Ipso has attracted controversy. In April last year, the regulator was accused of undermining free speech after reprimanding The Telegraph for quoting comments previously made in Parliament.

The watchdog upheld a complaint from the Muslim Association of Britain over allegations made in the House of Commons that it was “affiliated” to the Muslim Brotherhood, an organisation banned as a terrorist group in some countries.

Lord Gove, who was still an MP at the time, made the comment in the Commons under parliamentary privilege, which protects politicians against legal action by organisations or individuals for comments made in Parliament.

Ipso ruled that The Telegraph should have sought a response from the MAB, even though The Telegraph included in the article its rebuttal of Lord Gove’s allegations at the time he made them. Lord Gove said at the time that the ruling could stifle free speech.
