data.title: Five changes every landlord must prepare for in 2026
data.description:
Britain’s landlords must brace for a year of new rules, as the Government plans to enforce a raft of regulations and additional tax requirements on a sector already burdened by rising costs and tighter rules.

While the Government insists the changes are about raising standards and strengthening protection for tenants, landlords warn that it could lead to worse outcomes for renters if enough landlords decide to leave the market entirely.

Here, Telegraph Money sets out what’s coming for landlords this year and what you need to do to get prepared.

Making Tax Digital

When? From April 6.

What does it mean for landlords?

Making Tax Digital (MTD) will require certain taxpayers to report their income and expenses to HMRC four times a year, in addition to annual tax returns. This doesn’t only affect landlords – other taxpayers, such as the self-employed, are also affected.

For landlords specifically, MTD applies to those whose qualifying income exceeds £50,000, but this income doesn’t have to be from property. For example, if you have income from property and self-employment that is more than £50,000 combined, then you would need to register.

Those required to take action should have already received a letter from HMRC, but, as always, the onus is on you to check whether you should be signed up.

Heather Powell, of accountancy firm Blick Rothenberg, said: “The first return, reporting rental income and expenses for the three months from April 6, 2026 to July 5, 2026 has to be filed by Aug 7, 2026, with filing from ‘compliant software’ or bridging software that picks up the financial information from an Excel spreadsheet.”

As a landlord, you need to be on your game, as there is an initial £200 fine after two late filings.

While HMRC says this new filing regime will “increase the accuracy of filings”, the shift will undoubtedly feel daunting for the many landlords juggling multiple properties and income streams.

What’s more, the submissions must be made using MTD-compatible software. There is a long list of companies offering options, many of which require payment. Whether landlords try to grapple with this on their own or with the help of an accountant, they’re likely to incur additional costs.

The key right now is to find out whether you’re affected and, if you are, get your accounting records in order and start moving towards digital record-keeping.

Renters’ Rights Act reforms come into force

When? May 1.

What does it mean for landlords?

After becoming law in November, the first phase of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) 2025 reforms will come into effect from the start of May – and there’s a lot to get your head around.

Michael Dear, landlord insurance specialist at insurer Hiscox, said: “The RRA brings changes such as limits on rent increases and the end of fixed-term tenancies, which aim to make renting fairer.”

In addition, there are new rules on “no-fault” evictions and tenant requests to keep pets.

With only a few months to go, awareness is believed to be relatively high among landlords, but concerns remain that actual understanding is patchy.

Sián Hemming-Metcalfe, operations director at Inventory Base, a supplier, said: “Most landlords know the RRA is coming, but far fewer have grasped what it means in practice. This is not a cosmetic change; it rewrites how possession works, how rent is handled, how tenancies are structured and what landlords will need to evidence if something goes wrong.”

As such, there are a few things you should prepare now, if you haven’t already.

  • Before the abolition of Section 21: Review the new rules in detail, update your tenancy documentation, and potentially seek legal advice if you are likely to need to evict tenants.
  • Prepare for the end of fixed-term tenancies: All existing and new fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies must convert into rolling tenancies, so think now about how you need to change your existing processes and update your contract paperwork.
  • Prepare for rent increases being restricted to once per year: Now is a good time to assess how much rent you’re charging. Aneisha Beveridge, head of research at Hamptons, said: “With rent increases due to be open to tribunal challenge from May, many landlords are using the months ahead to ensure their rents are aligned as closely as possible to market levels.”
  • Plan for new pet rules: Requests to keep pets cannot be turned down “unreasonably” starting in May. Before this, it’s prudent to consider any practical adjustments that might better protect your property from pet damage, update your inventories and perhaps your tenancy agreement paperwork and insurance policy to make sure you’re covered.

For more information on what the new law means for landlords, see our full guide to the RRA.

Provide information to existing tenants

When? By May 31 to avoid penalties.

What does it mean for landlords?

This is part of the RRA, but has its own separate deadline, so we thought it was worth pointing out separately.

The requirement is for landlords to issue tenants with key information in writing, setting out the details of the tenancy, including – but not restricted to:

  • The property address
  • The tenancy start date
  • The rent amount and when it is due
  • A statement to confirm that any proposed rent rises will be in accordance with section 13 of the Housing Act 1988
  • Details of the tenancy deposit
  • Requirements should apply if either party wishes to end the tenancy.

New tenancies made on or after May 1 must include this information as standard. If you have existing tenancies – ones already in place on May 1 – you will need to issue this information to them if you haven’t already.

If it’s already part of their tenancy agreement, you must instead give your tenants a government-produced information sheet telling them how changes being made by the RRA might affect the tenancy. This is expected to be published this month and can be sent as a hard or digital copy.

Don’t ignore this, as failure to comply will be considered a breach of the act and can mean hefty penalties, starting from £4,000.

You can read the full government guidance for landlords here.

Further ahead: New Energy Performance Certificate metrics

When? Had been planned for the end of 2026, but recently delayed to 2027.

What does it mean for landlords?

Under current plans, landlords must ensure that all rental properties have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of “C” or above by 2030 or spend £10,000 to try.

However, the existing EPC system is also due for reform, with the single metric to be replaced by four new metrics, which had been expected in the latter half of this year. The Government has since announced the new system will not be introduced until 2027. 

Once introduced, landlords will have to achieve a rating of “C” in the property’s “fabric performance” (which considers insulation) and least two of the other three new green metrics.

The aim is to help landlords (as well as other homeowners and renters) understand certain headline measures in their properties. The assessments will also cover the heating system (encouraging the adoption of heat pumps), the building’s “smart-readiness” (the potential to integrate smart technology) and practical steps to improve homes and reduce energy costs.

The idea is that this will provide a broader picture of a property’s energy profile.

The Government estimates that the average landlord will need to spend £5,400 to meet the standard, so if you know your rental property or properties have a poor EPC rating, it’s worth planning now.

You may need to start setting money aside to fund improvements and start looking into grants you can apply for (such as the Boiler Upgrade Scheme if you are considering a heat pump).

Jason Harris-Cohen, managing director at LandlordBuyer, said: “This represents a significant retrofit challenge for landlords, many of whom face difficult decisions between absorbing costly upgrade expenses, raising rents, or exiting the market altogether.”

Minimum qualifications for letting agents

When? TBC.

What does it mean for landlords?

Last year, the Government set out proposals to introduce mandatory qualifications for letting agents to raise professional standards across the sector.

The plans include a new code of practice establishing minimum requirements for all residential property agents, including estate agents, managing agents and letting agents.

As yet, no date has been confirmed. However, with many landlords reliant on agents to meet increasingly complex obligations, there is growing pressure for clarity on when the reforms will take effect.

Until this happens, any landlords using letting agents to manage their properties should ensure that the agents are trained and prepared for the forthcoming law changes. For instance, is there a named compliance specialist available to answer questions about the new rules? Are there facilities for staff to be trained on the changes and any updates? Are they updating their services and terms in accordance with the new rules?

If not, you might want to consider moving to a different company, as a letting agent’s failure to follow the new law will embroil you as the landlord.

data.title: Virgin Media boss: I regret hitting customers with surprise price rises
data.description:
The chief executive of Virgin Media O2 said he regrets hitting mobile customers with surprise price rises.

The price rises triggered an exodus of customers and a rebuke from Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor.

Lutz Schüler acknowledged that increasing bills for people in the middle of their contracts “wasn’t the best decision”, adding that he had “not expected that reaction”.

Speaking to The Telegraph at Mobile World Congress in Barcelona, he said: “It is all about the customer, and that was not customer-friendly, and the market has reacted to it.

“We have learned, and therefore we were also happy to commit to government at the round table that we won’t do it again.”

O2 sparked a backlash in October when it announced that mobile bills would rise by £2.50 a month, equivalent to £30 a year, from next month. That is 70p – or 40pc – higher than the £1.80 increase customers were informed of when they signed up to their contracts.

The surprise move prompted interventions by Ms Reeves and Liz Kendall, the Technology Secretary, who asked the regulator Ofcom to review pricing in the telecoms sector.

Martin Lewis, the consumer rights campaigner, said O2 had made a “mockery” of new rules forcing providers to tell customers how much their bills will increase in pounds and pence, rather than using complex inflation-linked figures. Those rules were designed to improve transparency, but in reality have simply locked in inflation-busting price increases across the industry.

Virgin Media O2 (VMO2), alongside rivals including BT, VodafoneThree and Sky, last month signed up to further voluntary commitments on price rises as part of a new government charter.

But customers have voted with their feet. O2 lost 165,000 customers in the last three months of the year. Schüler said his company attributed around 110,000 of those directly to the price increases.

Global competition

While the German executive acknowledged that wrong-footing customers was a mistake, he maintained that mobile and broadband bills in the UK remained good value relative to global standards and argued that further increases were necessary to help tackle patchy coverage.

He said: “In other continents, the relative spending for connectivity is higher. Also, 20 years ago in Europe, it was higher. So if you want to strike the balance between investment and customer protection, I think we lost the balance a bit.”

Schüler warned that Britain was falling behind international peers in terms of economic growth and, in particular, in the rapidly developing field of AI.

While he admitted “that ship has sailed” when it came to Britain competing with major chatbot makers such as OpenAI, the VMO2 boss argued that there were opportunities for telecoms providers to cash in on the new technology.

He cited the example of US giant Verizon, which provides private networks to major companies specifically designed to handle high-capacity AI workloads.

“I hate the fact that Verizon is more advanced here than we are,” Schüler said. “And the only difference is they have more money to invest, as simple as that.

“I think we’ve been through a period which has been pretty brutal for European telecommunications companies. Look at the share prices. I hope that we wake up in the UK and use some levers we have to gain back a bigger part of the value chain.”

O2 has said it will spend £700m on upgrading its mobile network this year and has become the first operator in Europe to launch a satellite mobile service. However, it faces competition from a growing number of smaller rivals that piggyback off the major networks.

Fintech firms Revolt and Klarna have both launched mobile services, while energy challenger Octopus is weighing a similar move.

Competition will be further fuelled by the regulator’s insistence that VodafoneThree offer competitive wholesale prices to so-called mobile virtual network operators for three years as a condition of its £15bn merger.

He said: “That means in the next couple of years we have to invest more in our mobile network to stay competitive with VodafoneThree and at the same time it paves the way for the Revoluts of this world to offer insane, cheap prices. That is not a long-term sustainable solution.”

Future of VMO2

VMO2, which is approaching the fifth anniversary of its formation through a £31bn merger by parent companies Telefónica and Liberty Global, is attempting to turn the corner after a turbulent period.

In addition to the price rise controversy, VMO2 has suffered from slowing broadband revenues amid competition from a sprawling array of so-called “altnet” rivals. The company last month warned it expects revenues and profits to decline by up to 5pc this year.

The telecoms group is now rekindling efforts to become the biggest competitor to BT in a cut-throat broadband market.

VMO2, which has 5.8 million broadband customers, completed a £2bn takeover of Netomnia, the second-largest altnet, last month. The complex deal, conducted by Telefónica and Liberty Global alongside French private equity firm InfraVia Capital, will take the company’s total network access to around 20 million homes.

The takeover could fire the starting gun on a wave of consolidation across the sector. Many inadequate rivals that cropped up during an era of cheap borrowing are now straining under huge piles of debt. London-focused altnet G.Network was placed into administration in January, just weeks after being snapped up by vulture capital firm FitzWalter.

The £2bn price tag for Netomnia has been viewed as toppy by some industry analysts, while CityFibre, the UK’s largest altnet, cried foul on competition grounds, warning against an “ineffective duopoly” of BT and VMO2.

Schüler said the deal was an “offensive” move that would strengthen the company’s challenge to BT’s network division Openreach, while arguing that mergers were needed as many debt-laden altnets teeter on the brink of collapse.

“The altnet market as it is today is not sustainable, so consolidation needs to happen. And here we are. We do that consolidation,” Schüler said. “We are creating more competition because we are becoming the biggest challenger to Openreach.”

The fighting talk comes even as an uneasy relationship between VMO2’s parent companies has cast doubts over the future of the joint venture.

Telefónica, which is partly owned by the Spanish government, was thrown into chaos last year following the dramatic ousting of José María Álvarez-Pallete, its long-serving chief executive. His departure is widely thought to have been driven by Pedro Sánchez, Spain’s socialist prime minister, who instead appointed Blackburn-born executive Marc Murtra to the top job.

Murtra forced VMO2 to ditch its plans for a new wholesale business to challenge BT. It had been aiming to raise £1bn by selling a stake. Telefónica was then reported to be exploring plans to buy out Liberty Global’s 50pc stake in VMO2, though any deal would be complicated by the UK company’s £22bn debt pile.

Schüler, who is now Britain’s longest-serving telecoms boss, is sanguine about such machinations.

“I think from the outside it looked a bit more disruptive than it was,” he said. “My two shareholders are aligned on strategy. They now do this Netomnia deal together, and I think we end up in a pretty steady situation.”

data.title: Jo Malone sued for using her own name on perfume
data.description:
American cosmetics giant Estée Lauder has sued Jo Malone for breach of contract after the perfume tycoon used her own name on a new line of fragrances.

Ms Malone sold her eponymous perfume brand to Estée Lauder in 1999, along with the rights to use her name in any future business.

The US company has now filed a lawsuit against Ms Malone, her new company Jo Loves and the clothing retail chain Zara. It alleges trademark infringement and passing off, where a business misrepresents its goods or services as being those of a competitor.

The lawsuit, which was first reported by The Financial Times, relates to a range of perfumes launched last year in collaboration with Zara. A description of the fragrances on Zara’s website describes them as “created by Jo Malone CBE, founder of Jo Loves”.

Ms Malone has described selling the rights to her name as her “only regret”, adding that she still struggles to come to terms with the decision.

The entrepreneur stepped down as creative director of the company she founded in 2006 following a battle with cancer. She set up Jo Loves, which makes perfumes, candles and body lotions, in 2011.

An Estée Lauder spokesman said Ms Malone had agreed to “clear contractual terms” blocking her from using her name in certain commercial contexts, including the marketing of fragrances.

The company told the FT: “Ms Malone’s use of the name ‘Jo Malone’ in connection with recent commercial ventures goes beyond that legal agreement and undermines Jo Malone London’s unique brand equity.

“We respect Ms Malone’s right to pursue new opportunities. But legally binding contractual obligations cannot be disregarded and when those terms are breached, we will protect the brand that we have invested in and built over decades.”

Ms Malone is not the only cosmetics entrepreneur to sell away the rights to her own name.

Bobbi Brown sold her namesake company to Estée Lauder in 1995 and has since launched a new brand called Jones Road. Handbag designer Kate Spade legally changed her name to Kate Valentine after selling her intellectual property rights to Liz Claiborne.

The lawsuit against Ms Malone, Jo Loves and Zara comes a month after Estée Lauder filed a lawsuit against Walmart accusing the US supermarket giant of selling counterfeit Clinique, Aveda and Tom Ford beauty products.

The cosmetics giant described Walmart’s behaviour as “extreme, outrageous, fraudulent ... despicable and harmful”.

Walmart said it had “zero tolerance” for counterfeit products.

Jo Malone, Jo Loves and Zara were contacted for comment.

data.title: How Barclays got burnt by shadow banking
data.description:
Last October, Barclays boss CS Venkatakrishnan found himself in the crosshairs of financial analysts eager to grasp the bank’s exposure to the opaque world of shadow banking.

Just weeks earlier, the British bank had suffered a £110m loss on loans made to a little-known American car lender Tricolor, which had collapsed amid fraud allegations.

Now, with the analysts scrambling to understand Barclays’ exposure to other ventures, the 60-year-old banker was forced on the back foot to defend the bank’s private credit push.

“Credit is credit,” he shot back, after being grilled on the bank’s risk controls. “We’ve got strong risk management practices. We’re comfortable and confident with that.”

Yet now those words are under greater scrutiny after Barclays once again found itself on the losing end of another private credit foray.

Last week, Market Financial Solutions (MFS), a British-based shadow bank specialising in property finance, went under amid allegations of fraud.

Again, Barclays found itself on the hook for the collapse, with a reported exposure of between £500m and £600m. The Bank of England has been grilling Barclays and other lenders to MFS about the due diligence and risk assessments conducted before lending to the failed shadow bank.

The two blow-ups, as well as the banks’ intrinsic ties to private credit investors, have raised questions about Barclays’ foray into the risky, unregulated world of shadow banking – and whether it is giving it an unwanted headache.

In total, the British bank has a reported exposure of roughly £20bn to the private credit sector.

Venkatakrishnan, whose pay topped £15m last year, recently said that this was “relatively small” in contrast to the £346bn of loans currently issued to consumers and business customers across the bank.

The bank’s decision to move into shadow banking to juice returns is not surprising. Shadow banking, a loose term to describe private credit and non-bank lending, has mushroomed into a $2tn (£1.5tn) industry in recent years.

Since the last financial crisis, private equity firms have morphed into the world’s largest shadow banks, including Ares Management, Blackstone, Apollo Global Management and The Carlyle Group.

These companies step in to lend to companies when banks are unable to. Since the financial crisis, punishing capital rules mean banks like Barclays must set aside some of their money in a “buffer” for loans they make in case things go wrong.

Shadow banks are free from these constraints and can therefore make riskier – and ultimately more profitable – loans.

Yet for many traditional banks, including Barclays, the lure of doing business with this lucrative world has become too hard to resist.

Many of the links between mainstream banks and private credit providers have become fiendishly complex, an alphabet soup of products including credit default swaps (CDS) and Net Asset Value (NAV) loans.

Synthetic risk transfers

And Barclays has also become a major player in one of the more niche corners of this opaque world: synthetic risk transfers (SRTs).

These are esoteric transactions banks use to offload risk from their books, and Barclays is among the biggest players in Europe.

Since the financial crisis, banks have been hemmed in by rules requiring them to hold more capital to offset the risk of the loans they issue. SRTs were designed as something of a way around these rules.

In a typical SRT, a bank takes the riskiest slice of a pool of mortgages or company loans and offloads it to a shadow bank, which assumes the risk that the loans might default and is paid a hefty fee by the bank for doing so.

In exchange, the bank is allowed to hold less capital against the loans, since, in theory, the default risk has fallen.

Intended to free up cash for banks, Barclays has sold vast swathes of its loan book to the shadow banks over the past 10 years since it founded Project Colonnade, the bank’s programme for managing its growing appetite for SRTs.

Barclays has a total exposure to SRTs of an astonishing £50bn and has used these transactions to offload risk on about 45pc of its corporate loan book, according to Autonomous Research.

Growing concern

However, there is growing unease amongst regulators, with fears that these exotic trades resemble the sort of financial alchemy that precipitated the financial crisis.

Watchdogs have weighed in on the practice, from Britain’s Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) to the International Monetary Fund.

In the UK, each transaction must be approved by the PRA individually to qualify for lower capital treatment. Earlier this year, the European Central Bank even introduced a “fast-track” approval process for standardised SRTs.

“Like many other banks, we use risk transfers to help manage credit risk responsibly and make efficient use of capital, while complying with all regulatory requirements,” a Barclays spokesman said. “We disclose details on this activity transparently each quarter, along with our published results.”

However, watchdogs are worried about how exactly these alternative investors are raising the money to buy the slices of risky loans in the first place.

In some cases, private credit funds borrow from other banks to finance purchases. Some regulators fear that, by doing this, banks can hold less capital, while the risks associated with these loans never leave the banking system.

Instead, it boomerangs between banks, while shadowy private credit providers sit in the middle of the transaction, obscuring who exactly owes what.

“The risk hasn’t disappeared from the system; it has just moved from highly regulated, transparent banks to lightly regulated, opaque private credit funds,” said Prof Raghavendra Rau, from Cambridge Judge Business School.

“How much exposure the regulated banks have to lightly regulated non-bank financial institutions is opaque. And worse, because private credit lacks public market discipline, the losses can be magnified, meaning unexpected shocks could cascade through the financial system rapidly before regulators even see them coming.”

Steven Hall, from KPMG, said: “It’s worth noting that we’re talking about risk transfer here rather than risk removal. So when we say we’ve transferred the risk from the bank’s balance sheet, it hasn’t just disappeared, it’s gone somewhere,”

Amid scrutiny, some banks have shied away from providing funding to shadow banks for this purpose – known as financing SRTs.

Deutsche Bank stepped away from the market at the start of last year. Barclays, which was never a significant player in the space, allowed its operations to wind down completely.

While financing SRTs has been the focus of regulatory intrigue, that doesn’t mean the banks on the other side of these transactions, like Barclays, that offloaded the risk in the first place, are completely out of the woods.

“The key risk here is that if markets crash when an SRT matures, banks won’t be able to renew their protection,” said Rau. “This loan may be protected (it depends on the insurer) – but they will not be able to renew.

“That means that their capital requirements will suddenly spike, forcing them to freeze lending when the economy needs it most.”

Last month, as Barclays again braved questions from curious analysts, SRTs and shadow banks were again top of mind for financiers keen to understand the British bank.

“We do really think about SRTs as a risk management tool,” said Daniel Fairclough, Barclays’ group treasurer. “It’s about the areas where we’re growing, where we think the risk is higher, and we want to manage that.”

With customers, City analysts and regulators prying into Barclays’ links to the shadow banks, Venkatakrishnan will be hoping there are no more blow-ups to come.

data.title: Jo Malone sued for using her own name on perfume
data.description:
American cosmetics giant Estée Lauder has sued Jo Malone for breach of contract after the perfume tycoon used her own name on a new line of fragrances.

Ms Malone sold her eponymous perfume brand to Estée Lauder in 1999, along with the rights to use her name in any future business.

The US company has now filed a lawsuit against Ms Malone, her new company Jo Loves and the clothing retail chain Zara. It alleges trademark infringement and passing off, where a business misrepresents its goods or services as being those of a competitor.

The lawsuit, which was first reported by The Financial Times, relates to a range of perfumes launched last year in collaboration with Zara. A description of the fragrances on Zara’s website describes them as “created by Jo Malone CBE, founder of Jo Loves”.

Ms Malone has described selling the rights to her name as her “only regret”, adding that she still struggles to come to terms with the decision.

The entrepreneur stepped down as creative director of the company she founded in 2006 following a battle with cancer. She set up Jo Loves, which makes perfumes, candles and body lotions, in 2011.

An Estée Lauder spokesman said Ms Malone had agreed to “clear contractual terms” blocking her from using her name in certain commercial contexts, including the marketing of fragrances.

The company told the FT: “Ms Malone’s use of the name ‘Jo Malone’ in connection with recent commercial ventures goes beyond that legal agreement and undermines Jo Malone London’s unique brand equity.

“We respect Ms Malone’s right to pursue new opportunities. But legally binding contractual obligations cannot be disregarded and when those terms are breached, we will protect the brand that we have invested in and built over decades.”

Ms Malone is not the only cosmetics entrepreneur to sell away the rights to her own name.

Bobbi Brown sold her namesake company to Estée Lauder in 1995 and has since launched a new brand called Jones Road. Handbag designer Kate Spade legally changed her name to Kate Valentine after selling her intellectual property rights to Liz Claiborne.

The lawsuit against Ms Malone, Jo Loves and Zara comes a month after Estée Lauder filed a lawsuit against Walmart accusing the US supermarket giant of selling counterfeit Clinique, Aveda and Tom Ford beauty products.

The cosmetics giant described Walmart’s behaviour as “extreme, outrageous, fraudulent ... despicable and harmful”.

Walmart said it had “zero tolerance” for counterfeit products.

Jo Malone, Jo Loves and Zara were contacted for comment.

data.title: Elon Musk’s Tesla to start selling energy to British households
data.description:
Elon Musk’s Tesla has been given the green light to launch a household energy supplier in Britain despite Ed Miliband’s fierce opposition to the billionaire.

Ofgem has granted Tesla a licence allowing it to supply electricity to households and businesses across the country.

The licence, granted to its subsidiary Tesla Energy Ventures, took effect on Wednesday following a seven-month assessment by the energy regulator.

The approval paves the way for the launch of Tesla Energy, a household energy supplier that is likely to be closely integrated with Mr Musk’s electric car maker and home battery storage product Powerwall.

The company already runs an electricity provider in Texas that enables Tesla owners to charge their cars cheaply and sell any excess energy back to the grid.

But Mr Musk’s swoop on Britain will put the mercurial billionaire in direct competition with the likes of Octopus and British Gas.

The approval by Ofgem comes despite a public rebuke of the Tesla tycoon by Britain’s Energy Secretary.

Mr Miliband last year accused Mr Musk of being a key player in a network of individuals seeking to “destroy the ties that bind our communities and our way of life”.

Speaking at the Labour Party conference, he said: “We have a message for Elon Musk: get the hell out of our politics and our country.”

Tesla’s energy supplier plans have been in the works for a long time. The company was granted a separate electricity generation licence in 2020 and has since been hiring operations and policy staff to prepare for the launch of its retail service.

The EV maker applied for an electricity supply licence from Ofgem in July last year. However, it has not applied for a gas supply licence, meaning most UK households would have to choose a different gas provider.

Tesla must now comply with Ofgem’s licence conditions, including rules around treating customers fairly, financial responsibility and information provision.

The regulator will monitor compliance and has the power to fine the company or restrict its licence if it falls foul of the rules.

An Ofgem spokesman said: “Protecting consumers and maintaining a secure, fair energy market underpins every licensing decision we make.

“Following a detailed assessment, Ofgem has concluded that Tesla Energy Ventures Limited meets all statutory requirements to be licensed as an energy supplier and will be subject to the same binding obligations, monitoring and enforcement as all other suppliers from day one.

“We monitor compliance continuously and will not hesitate to use our powers where standards are not met.” 

Tesla, which has sold more than a quarter of a million cars in the UK, is well-placed to shake up the energy market.

In addition to its cars, the company has sold tens of thousands of Powerwall batteries to British homes, while it also sells home chargers for electric cars.

This gives the company a large existing customer base to tap into. It could also seek to capitalise on so-called “vehicle to home” technology currently under trial that uses a car’s battery to power the home.

But the launch also comes amid a sales slump for Tesla, which is grappling with softening demand and fierce competition from cut-price Chinese rivals.

The company has also suffered from a consumer backlash against Mr Musk over his support for Donald Trump, his work on the White House’s now-disbanded Department of Government Efficiency (Doge) cost-cutting project, and his enthusiasm for far-right political figures, including Germany’s AfD and Tommy Robinson.

Tesla sold 2,208 vehicles in the UK last month, down more than 45pc from a year earlier, according to data from New Automotive.

data.title: Revolut gets long-delayed banking licence after Reeves row
data.description:
Revolut has been awarded a full UK banking licence, ending a five-year wait marred by accusations of political interference from Rachel Reeves.

The $75bn (£56bn) fintech start-up said the Bank of England’s Prudential Regulation Authority had lifted restrictions on its probationary licence, allowing it to launch as a regulated bank in Britain for the first time.

The licence will enable Revolut to make loans and for customer deposits to be protected under the Government’s Financial Services Compensation Scheme.

Revolut first applied for a banking licence in 2021. Approval dragged on for far longer than normal, in part because of questions over the company’s financial accounting.

The delay led Nikolay Storonsky, its chief executive, to criticise UK regulators.

It emerged that Ms Reeves had sought to broker a meeting between the company and City regulators last year.

The meeting was blocked by Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, amid concerns that it could be seen as meddling with the licence process.

The Treasury said at the time it supported the Bank’s operational independence.

The Bank had told the Treasury in 2023 that it planned to reject Revolut’s application, before resuming discussions that led to the licence being granted.

It came after auditors issued a “qualified opinion” on the company’s accounts in 2023, saying it was unable to verify a portion of its revenues. The accounting issues were subsequently reconciled.

The company was allowed to enter the “mobilisation” stage, a preliminary step to a full licence, in July 2024, but its wait for this to turn into a full licence has taken longer than any major financial company.

Revolut has 13 million customers in Britain and is the country’s most valuable start-up. A UK licence is likely to act as a springboard for applications in other countries.

Revolut said it would start to offer customers licenced current accounts in the coming days before moving a wider group in the coming weeks.

It currently offers accounts under electronic money institution permissions, which do not offer the same protections for customers, and cannot be used to fund lending.

Mr Storonsky said the licence marked a “significant moment for the company”.

“Launching our UK bank has been a long-term strategic priority for Revolut, and marks a significant moment in our journey,” he said.

He added: “The UK is our home market and central to our growth. We look forward to introducing a full suite of banking services to our millions of UK customers.

“This is a vital step in our mission to build the world’s first truly global bank.”

The Bank of England and Treasury did not comment.

Revolut said it had applied for a US national bank charter last week that would allow it to offer loans and credit cards.

Mr Storonsky, one of Britain’s richest men, recently switched his official residency back to the UK after filings suggested he had moved to Dubai.

A disclosure last year that suggested he had changed his residency to the United Arab Emirates had drawn questions from regulators examining the banking licence application.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: The politically incorrect humour of Viz magazine will rise again
data.description:
Do you remember Viz magazine? I certainly do. In fact I can remember the first time I saw it. I was drinking with friends in a Soho pub back in the Eighties and these funny Geordie lads came around the saloon bar. They were selling, by hand, this quite amateurish-looking rag.

The normal Soho pub reaction in this situation is to wave away the hustlers wearily – but there was something about the amiable self-deprecation of these blokes from the Toon. “Please buy our magazine, it’s new, if you don’t think it’s funny, we’ll give you your money back. Not really.”

So I bought a copy, and the Geordie jokers went on their hustling way. And within an hour I was doubled up with laughter. Because the affable Novocastrian chancers were not kidding: their inky little magazine, a melange of comic strips, fake news, hoax ads, and “readers’ letters”, was profoundly funny. Funnier – to this day – than any magazine I’ve read.

The comic strip characters alone were hilarious. Big Vern, the cockney gangster constantly shooting himself in innocuous situations so as to avoid chokey: “I ain’t doing bird, Harry!”. The lecherous drooling baker yearning to molest his self-made gingerbread strumpets. Or The Pathetic Sharks refusing to attack stricken swimmers because they were scared of an injured dorsal fin.

It was also pleasingly surreal. I loved Black Bag, the adventures of the Faithful Border Bin Liner, a spoof of Enid Blyton crossed with Dada. Meanwhile, the letters page was clearly written by the editors, especially the Top Tips readerly advice, e.g. “instead of buying an expensive personalised number plate, why not buy an ordinary one and change your name to C762 JXR?”

Pretty soon Viz went viral. At its peak, as noted on the front page of The Telegraph in March 5 1991, it was selling over a million copies a month, and excellent characters like Roger Mellie (the presciently lascivious “Man on the Telly”) actually got turned into proper TV. There were various other spin-offs, and there was money-spinning merchandise, and all of this was fine, because one, Viz was still funny, and two, Viz annoyed the right people.

One such was an acquaintance of mine, a mildly famous, very feminist academic, not overly blessed with a sense of humour. She denounced Viz as scurrilous and vile because it dared to lampoon sexually liberated but somewhat overweight Tyneside women in one notorious strip: The Fat Slags.

I did my best to defend Viz. I told my friend that yes, The Fat Slags was a little cruel, but it was also not without affection. And besides, Viz attacked everyone: from pathetically horny but failed Geordie men (Sid the Sexist) to liberals with hairy legs (The Modern Parents).

Sadly, my humourless acquaintance – with the assistance of the internet – went on to win this particular culture war. After its glory years, Viz slipped from view and all but vanished. A more po-faced Britain had, apparently, lost its taste for Viz’s rude, incorrect, hilarious barbs, preferring the dreary, predictable pap that passes for modern comedy. By which I mean things like Have I Got News For You. Have you seen this show recently? It is now less satire, more earnest staff room meeting. It is also a weekly funeral for British humour.

And yet, and yet. I wonder if Viz is really dead, or if it lies sleeping in a hidden place: like King Arthur, waiting to return and save Britain with its superbly salty British wit. Every so often, in a WhatsApp group chat, or bantering down the pub, I will hear someone crack a sharp, daring, brilliant and surreal gag, and I will think: “There, that’s Viz humour.” It still exists, but it exists in the shadows, away from disapproving eyes and ears. Like samizdat literature in Soviet Russia.

That’s why I think a version of Viz will return, because, in the end, you can’t keep a good joke down. Somewhere out there, right now, I bet there’s a group of lads and lasses in Perth or Peckham looking at our beloved, sad, rainy, ridiculous country and thinking: that’s funny. Let’s laugh at it.

data.title: Labour poised to raise energy bills to save Britain’s factories
data.description:
Labour is poised to impose higher energy bills on households and businesses as ministers scramble to cut electricity costs for Britain’s struggling factories.

Officials form the Department for Business and Trade have met energy suppliers in recent weeks to discuss how a £1bn support scheme for heavy industry will be rolled out.

Sir Keir Starmer announced the scheme last year as part of Labour’s industrial strategy, which will cut the electricity bills of around 7,000 energy-intensive companies including carmakers, aircraft factories and chemical plants.

Britain’s businesses are subject to the highest electricity prices in the developed world, which manufacturers have warned is threatening their future.

The British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS) will increase the discount on electricity network charges for energy-intensive industries to 90pc from 60pc currently. Companies will be exempt from paying levies including the renewables obligation, the feed-in tariff and the capacity market.

Sources familiar with the talks said there was a “working assumption” that it will be paid for through charges passed on to consumers through their bills.

This is despite government pledges that the funds would come from cutting costs elsewhere in the energy system.

Any increase to energy bills will be controversial at a time when the Iran war has sent oil and gas prices soaring – with analysts warning it could push the typical British household’s bill to £2,500 a year.

‘Deep frustration in the sector’

Some of the cost, estimated by manufacturing body MakeUK to be £1bn per year, is also likely to be paid for through reforms to wind and solar farm subsidies which will cut payments to generators, the sources said.

Meeting notes seen by The Telegraph suggest the plans are in the early stages and are not yet confirmed government policy. It is also uncertain whether households, businesses or both will ultimately shoulder the costs.

A Government spokesman on Friday did not deny the claims but said further details would be published soon.

A proposal to add yet more levies to bills is likely to provoke a backlash from the energy industry as well, after companies warned that the litany of “non-commodity costs” piled on to consumers over the years was now the main factor driving bills higher.

Adam Berman, head of policy at Energy UK, which represents suppliers, said: “The Government has repeatedly offered assurances that the BICS scheme will not be funded through energy bills.

“There will be deep frustration in the sector if this is not the case, and that yet another levy is being added to energy bills pushing up costs for homes and businesses across the country.

“Support for energy intensive and trade exposed businesses with their energy bills is critical, but it can’t come at the expense of the rest of the economy.”

Talks of new levy

Sources said the way BICS has been structured – which resembles another industrial power subsidy – strongly suggested it would be funded by a new levy of some kind.

This would most likely be charged to suppliers, who would then pass the costs to households and businesses through the unit rate for electricity.

It is thought it could add at least £5 per year to a typical household’s bill if implemented, further undercutting a £150 reduction pledged by ministers this spring which has already been reduced to £117 by rising network charges.

On Friday, a source familiar with the discussions said: “The Government is talking to suppliers about how to take this off people’s bills.

“It looks like they will take powers to levy suppliers – who will then pass that cost on to customers.”

They warned that charging consumers for the scheme was akin to “raising Peter’s levies to lower Paul’s” and said it was coming as “big cost rises from generator and network companies” are also expected.

Another person involved in the talks added: “There is a working assumption that, at least in part, the Government will have to create a new levy on bills to subsidise BICS.”

data.title: Iran war risks inflating prices of laptops and phones
data.description:
Laptops and phones could become more expensive after the war in Iran threatened the global supply of crucial semiconductors.

Soaring energy costs could cripple the energy-intensive chip industry, while supplies of key elements required for manufacturing such as helium and bromide could also hit supply.

Politicians and companies in Taiwan and South Korea have warned that a prolonged crisis could affect production, while analysts said the energy shock could raise prices.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), the world’s biggest semiconductor manufacturer, accounts for 9pc of Taiwan’s entire electricity demand.

The island democracy relies on Qatar, which has suspended exports of natural gas, for a third of its liquefied natural gas consumption.

The fossil fuel, which is also supplied by Australia, provides around half of Taiwan’s electricity. Meanwhile, supplies of crucial chip-making elements have been threatened by the war.

Qatar is one of the key sources of helium, which is used to cool silicon wafers during chip manufacturing. Meanwhile, Israel is one of the world’s leading producers of bromine, which is used when etching circuits on silicon.

The supply pressures threaten to add to soaring chip prices, which have already been rising because of demand from AI data centres.

South Korea and Taiwan have sought to downplay the impact of the war in Iran, pointing to adequate supplies.

An industry insider told the Kyunghyang Shinmun newspaper in Seoul that there were “substantial existing stockpiles of helium and other materials”, while Taiwan’s minister of economic affairs said the country has 11 days of natural gas reserves.

TSMC has said it does not anticipate an immediate impact but is monitoring the situation. However, a longer conflict could threaten supplies.

MS Hwang, of Counterpoint Research, said: “At present, the supply of raw materials and the rise in energy prices do not have a major impact on semiconductor manufacturing.

“The increase in energy prices can be considered a factor behind rising semiconductor prices due to higher manufacturing costs, but the impact is limited.”

However, he added: “The geopolitical instability underlying the rise in energy prices could cause difficulties in securing raw materials such as gases needed for semiconductor manufacturing.

“If chip production does not increase as expected due to these challenges, semiconductor prices may rise, directly translating into a burden for customers.”

The share prices of Samsung and SK Hynix, Korea’s two major memory suppliers, have fallen by 12pc since the start of the conflict, while TSMC’s shares have fallen by 7pc.

TSMC warned last year that “increased prices for electricity could increase TSMC’s manufacturing costs”.

Conversely, soaring energy prices and disruption in the Middle East could affect demand for memory components from energy-intensive AI data centres.

Surging spending on data centres has led chip manufacturers to divert memory supplies to AI, leading to increased prices for consumers buying phones, laptops and video game consoles.

data.title: How Trump’s attacks on Iran are fuelling Putin’s war machine
data.description:
As the spiralling price of oil threatens to unleash a new wave of inflationary pressure, one person is profiting from an extremely fortuitous payday: Vladimir Putin.

Since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran at the end of February, the price of Brent crude has soared by 20pc to $87 (£64.7). At one point, it even surpassed $117, its highest level since the 2022 energy crisis.

Unsurprisingly, this has unleashed chaos across global markets, with stocks sinking and government borrowing costs soaring.

The situation is damaging almost everyone, apart from the Kremlin.

According to Ben Harris, the director of economic studies at the Brookings Institution and the former chief economist at the Treasury, the oil shock triggered by the conflict is “a massive benefit to Russia”.

As one of the world’s largest producers of oil and gas, Russia generates huge profits from higher oil and gas prices.

Roughly speaking, every $1 increase in the price of oil, if sustained over a whole year, increases Russia’s annual oil export revenues by $2.7bn, says Benjamin Hilgenstock of the Kyiv School of Economics (KSE) Institute.

Before the conflict began, Goldman Sachs forecast average Brent crude prices for this year at $64.

Based on the KSE Institute’s rule of thumb, if oil prices are maintained across the year at their current $87 level, this would boost the Kremlin’s annual oil export revenues by $62bn – equivalent to a 39pc year-on-year boost.

Last week, analysts were betting that the war would last no more than a month, but the longer the conflict drags on, the bigger the squeeze on supply.

And the longer oil and gas production remains shut down, the more challenging it will be to return to normal production levels even once trade resumes.

Last year, Russia raked in $160bn from oil sales and $40bn from gas.

If European gas prices were maintained at an average of €100 per megawatt hour and Brent crude averaged $90 across 2026, then Russia’s oil and gas export revenues “might easily exceed $300bn per year”, says Borys Dodonov, head of the KSE’s centre for energy and climate studies.

Crucially, Russia benefits disproportionately from energy market turmoil because it erodes the effectiveness of Western sanctions on the Kremlin’s energy trade. And this latest market shock could not have come at a better time for Putin.

After nearly a year of taking no action on Russia, Trump began ramping up the pressure last October, when he announced secondary sanctions on banks that facilitated trade with Russian oil majors, Rosneft and Lukoil.

He also used tariffs on India to pressure Narendra Modi, its prime minister, to scale back purchases of Russian energy. At the same time, Europe and the UK also launched new sanctions.

“India had cut Russian imports almost in half,” says Craig Kennedy, a former vice chairman at Bank of America and expert in Russian oil and gas. “This forced Moscow to offer eye-watering discounts to clear inventory build-ups.”

Between October and January, the discount on Russian oil compared to Brent crude widened from $10 to $24, according to data from the International Energy Agency (IEA).

In percentage terms, this was the biggest gap since the US and Europe launched a price cap on Russian oil in Dec 2022.

This had an enormous impact on Russia’s oil export revenues, which slumped to just $11bn per month through November, December and January.

This was down 26pc on the same period a year earlier and the lowest monthly level since the start of the war in Ukraine in 2022.

Now, the war in Iran gives Russia an opportunity to claw back some of this cash.

“The discount is a reflection of the attractiveness of Russian oil versus the other oil,” says Hilgenstock.

“If the other oil is stuck somewhere because of the current situation, the Russian oil will become relatively more attractive. Presumably, the discount is going to decline.”

Oil and gas revenues are crucial for Russia’s war machine. Broadly speaking, for every $2 in oil and gas export revenues, the Kremlin collects $1 in taxes.

“The timing is awful,” says Harris. “Russia was starting to run a deficit, which would mean increased pain on the Russian government and the Russian people, and then this windfall comes around.”

Watered-down sanctions

In an effort to alleviate the oil shock, Trump is already watering down his sanctions against Russia.

On Thursday, the US issued a 30-day waiver allowing India to bypass sanctions and purchase Russian oil that is already at sea.

Russia has a record volume of oil on water, meaning oil that has been loaded onto tankers but is struggling to find a buyer. Some 9.5 million barrels are in Asian waters, which implies that the US has now given India the green light to buy.

Scott Bessent, the US treasury secretary, claimed the 30-day waiver is “deliberately short-term” and will not bring financial benefit to the Kremlin.

However, the move will be interpreted as a clear signal to Russia’s oil buyers that the White House is paring back on sanctions because officials are increasingly scared about soaring petrol prices.

Andreas Schröder, the head of energy analytics at Independent Commodity Intelligence Services (ICIS), says China is likely to ramp up its purchases of Russian crude oil to replace imports from Iran amid disruptions.

“Actors in this trade potentially think the US is otherwise occupied, and so they can get away with some interactions with Russian oil that they may have considered too risky a month or two ago,” says Hilgenstock.

The US has also waived sanctions indefinitely on the German arm of Rosneft, which operates the refinery that supplies Berlin with 90pc of its petrol.

If the war in the Middle East drags on, political appetite for sanctions could fall away.

“My sense is that as the oil price continues to rise, there’ll be some who feel now is not the time to put pressure on Russia to export less,” says Harris.

Jan-Eric Fähnrich, senior gas analyst at Rystad Energy, said last week that a prolonged cut-off in Qatari gas could raise discussion in Europe about lifting sanctions and reintroducing Russian gas to the market.

Putin is already playing on Europe’s energy market fears, suggesting last week that he could cut off the remaining gas supplies to the continent and sell it to more profitable markets elsewhere.

But this is a hollow threat, says Kennedy. “Moscow simply doesn’t have the infrastructure to move it to other markets, much less ones that can match European prices,” he says.

“If it did, it would have done so already.”

Nevertheless, the outlook is poor. “It reverses all of the positive outcomes that have come from this increasingly aggressive sanctioning effort by the EU and UK,” says Harris.

“There’s no way to cut it; it is just bad news for Ukraine.”
